title: Agent host MCP servers do not start automatically
Telemetry: `agentHost.turnCompleted.permissionLevel` is provider-specific and shouldn't live on the generic turn event
body:
### Problem

The `agentHost.turnCompleted` telemetry event (`IAgentHostTurnCompletedEvent` / `IAgentHostTurnCompletedClassification` in `src/vs/platform/agentHost/node/agentHostTelemetryReporter.ts`) includes a `permissionLevel` field. Unlike the rest of that event (`timeToFirstProgress`, `totalTime`, `result`, `model`), **`permissionLevel` is not generic across agent-host providers** ΓÇö it is only ever populated for Copilot AH and is empty for Claude and Codex AH.

### Root cause

The value is sourced in shared code from the platform `autoApprove` config key:

```ts
// src/vs/platform/agentHost/node/agentSideEffects.ts  (_getTurnTelemetryContext)
const permissionValue = state?.config?.values[SessionConfigKey.AutoApprove];
```

Only the Copilot provider writes `SessionConfigKey.AutoApprove`. The other harnesses model permissions/approvals with their own, namespaced config keys:

- **Claude** ΓÇö `ClaudeSessionConfigKey.PermissionMode` (`permissionMode`): `default` / `acceptEdits` / `bypassPermissions` / `plan` / `auto`
- **Codex** ΓÇö `CodexSessionConfigKey.ApprovalPolicy` (`codex.approvalPolicy`), plus `codex.sandboxMode`, `codex.networkAccessEnabled`, `codex.webSearchMode`, etc.

So the shared turn-completed path bakes in a Copilot-specific assumption (`autoApprove`), and `permissionLevel` comes back empty for every Claude/Codex turn.

### Why it doesn't belong on the generic event

`agentHost.turnCompleted` is otherwise a harness-agnostic *turn performance* event. `permissionLevel` is a *provider-specific config snapshot*, and there is no lossless normalization across harnesses ΓÇö Codex's `sandboxMode ├ù networkAccess` axes and Claude's single `permissionMode` axis don't map cleanly onto Copilot's `default` / `autoApprove` / `autopilot`. Putting one provider's config field on the shared classification makes the metric look generic when it isn't, and silently yields empty data for 2 of 3 providers.

### Proposed direction

- Remove `permissionLevel` from the shared `agentHost.turnCompleted` event so it stays purely harness-agnostic (timing / result / model).
- Emit permission/config telemetry from **provider-specific code** instead (e.g. `agentHost.copilot.*`), so each harness reports its own config faithfully and the values are accurate per provider. This mirrors the provider-namespaced pattern (e.g. the proposed `agentHost.claude.subagentResolved`).

Internal context / scorecard gap: microsoft/vscode-internalbacklog#8205.

### Code references

- `src/vs/platform/agentHost/node/agentHostTelemetryReporter.ts` ΓÇö `permissionLevel` on `IAgentHostTurnCompletedEvent` + classification
- `src/vs/platform/agentHost/node/agentSideEffects.ts` ΓÇö `_getTurnTelemetryContext` reads `SessionConfigKey.AutoApprove`
- `src/vs/platform/agentHost/node/agentHostTurnTracker.ts` ΓÇö plumbs `permissionLevel` through `turnStarted` ΓåÆ `turnCompleted`
- `src/vs/platform/agentHost/common/sessionConfigKeys.ts`, `common/claudeSessionConfigKeys.ts`, `node/codex/codexSessionConfigKeys.ts` ΓÇö the divergent per-provider key namespaces

title: Browser: Chat shows multiple stacked askQuestions requests to share existing browser tabs
Async subagents can be blocked
body:
Symptoms:

A session stops and says it's waiting for an async subagent to complete. The subagent never completes. If you check the events.jsonl "Open Copilot CLI State File", the last entry is a `permission.requested` that is never resolved.

The bug involves
- Main session is unsubscribed by client
- It goes idle while waiting for the async subagent
- It's evicted from AH memory
- Subagent completes and tries to wake up the main agent again
- This doesn't work when it has been evicted

This looks really similar to https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/323738 but they are not the same

title: Support Multi-Chat in Codex harness
body:
Summary

Add Multi-Chat support to the Codex harness so it matches the Copilot experience.

Background

- The Multi-Chat feature (previously called Sub-Sessions) is currently available for the Copilot harness and works in AHP sessions for Copilot.
- Team feedback and user studies have shown confusion around the feature and its name; the UX should be consistent across harnesses rather than Copilot-only.
- Codex AHP sessions do not currently show the Multi-Chat UI.

Proposal

- Implement Multi-Chat in the Codex harness with parity to the Copilot implementation and UX.
- Ensure Multi-Chat is available in AHP sessions for Codex as it is for Copilot.
- Minimize agent integration effort (sessions should be transparent to agents where possible), and document any agent-facing changes required.
- Update tests, e2e scenarios, and relevant documentation to reflect Multi-Chat availability in the Codex harness.

Acceptance criteria

- Multi-Chat UI appears and behaves the same in the Codex harness as it does in Copilot.
- Multi-Chat is available and functional in AHP sessions for Codex.
- Agent/handler code for Codex either requires no changes or any required changes are documented and minimal.
- Relevant tests and documentation updated.

Implementation notes

- Verify whether sessions need to be linked or if the agent surface can remain unchanged (transparent sessions).
- Coordinate UX changes with the Copilot harness team to ensure consistency and avoid repeating prior confusion around naming/behavior.
- Add testing steps to validate in manual and automated tests across the Codex harness and AHP sessions.

---

Split from #322776 (Claude). This issue tracks the Codex harness.

title: Invalid slash sub-command should respond with the valid options instead of a flat error
body:
Testing #323402

Running an unsupported sub-command, e.g. `/memory disable`, returns a bare "invalid command" error. The rejection is correct (`/memory` supports `on`/`off`/`show`, not `disable`), but the error is a dead end.

It would be much more helpful to **respond with the valid options** when a sub-command is unknown ΓÇö for example:
- "Unknown option 'disable'. Did you mean: `on`, `off`, `show`?"
- or re-trigger completions for that command's sub-commands so the user can pick a valid one
- bonus: suggest the closest match (`disable` ΓåÆ `off`)

Note: the validation likely comes from the Copilot CLI / SDK rather than VS Code's completion layer, so this may be an upstream item (similar to #323401).

title: Slash command completions don't appear when / is not at the start of an empty input
body:
Testing #323402

In a Copilot Agent Host session, slash command completions only appear when `/` is typed into a completely **empty** chat input. They do not trigger in these cases:

- Typing `/` in the **middle of a sentence** (when there is already text before the `/`).
- Typing `/` when there are **leading/trailing spaces** in the input (e.g. `  /` or text plus a space then `/`).

It would be helpful for completions to also trigger when `/` is typed mid-sentence or with surrounding spaces, so commands can be inserted without clearing/trimming the input first.

title: Slash command completions should indicate the source of each command
body:
Testing #323402

When typing `/` in a Copilot Agent Host session, the completion list shows the available slash commands but it's **hard to tell where each command comes from** ΓÇö e.g. built-in Copilot CLI commands vs. ones contributed by extensions/plugins, MCP, or custom agents. They all look the same.

It would help to **indicate the source/origin** of each slash command in the completions list (for example a label, group heading, or icon), so users can tell which commands are native vs. contributed and avoid confusion when names overlap.

title: Evaluate Agent Host custom terminal tool vs SDK shell tool for chat terminal UX parity
body:
For Agent Host Copilot sessions, would we be "Good" relying completely on CLI SDK's built-in shell tool, or continue to invest in custom terminal tool. The reason this matters: VS Code's terminal is not just stdout/stderr. It is where users look when a command is slow, hung, waiting for input, asking for a secret, starting a server, or doing something surprising. If Agent Host only relies on the SDK shell tool, commands may still run, but we may lose "tight integration" and bunch of UX flows that chat had with integrated terminal. Current switch: - `chat.agentHost.customTerminalTool.enabled=false`: Copilot SDK sessions rely on the SDK shell behavior. - `chat.agentHost.customTerminalTool.enabled=true`: Agent Host overrides the SDK built-in shell tools with VS Code/Agent Host terminal-backed tools. Important runtime context: Copilot CLI/runtime shell execution has moved away from `node-pty`-backed shell sessions toward direct `child_process.spawn` with stdio pipes. That is a good direction for predictable command output, but it also means the chat terminal experience can deviate quite a bit from our local agent harness atm. * Gaps are noted/listed down below in pros/cons section. Also awkward: AHP itself treats terminals as first-class subscribable resources: https://microsoft.github.io/agent-host-protocol/guide/terminals.html. What we need to decide: - Is the SDK/runtime shell tool enough for VS Code Agent Host sessions? - Do we need the Agent Host custom terminal tool for VS Code-quality terminal UX? - Or do we need a smaller SDK/API integration point so the SDK shell can still surface VS Code terminal affordances?
SDK shell path: what we gain and what we risk What we gain: - Good fit for one-shot, non-interactive commands where the model only needs stdout/stderr and an exit code. - Less VS Code-owned PTY lifecycle, terminal emulation, shell integration, resize, and platform-specific shell behavior. - Piped/non-TTY execution can avoid pager/color/control-sequence problems and is easier for the model to parse. - Better parity with other SDK hosts if everyone uses the same default shell tool. - Potentially simpler sandbox and telemetry ownership because execution stays in the SDK/runtime path. What we risk losing: - No real VS Code terminal to focus, reveal, scroll, copy from, or manually inspect. - In particular, a spawned non-interactive shell may not load the same startup files as a user's integrated terminal. For example, `zsh -c` typically does not load `.zshrc` or `.zprofile` (`.zprofile` is login-shell behavior, `.zshrc` is interactive-shell behavior), and non-interactive `bash -c` does not load `.bashrc` unless configured through `BASH_ENV`. There would also be no shell integration script that runs. - Shell execution may bypass AHP's first-class terminal resource model entirely: no terminal URI, no root terminal entry, no claim transfer, no `terminal/input`/`terminal/data` stream, no resize/control, and no command detection for the client to subscribe to. - No direct user input path for prompts, REPLs, sudo/password-like flows, CLIs asking questions, or commands that need Ctrl+C. - Shell/profile initialization may differ from the user's integrated terminal. A direct spawned shell may skip `.zshrc`, `.zprofile`, `.bashrc`, or tool-specific activation hooks unless the runtime explicitly asks for login/interactive shell behavior or injects an equivalent profile. - Less user trust when commands are slow, hung, or producing important output somewhere the user cannot inspect. - Accessibility risk: screen reader users may lose the familiar terminal surface and "terminal is awaiting input" workflow. - Weaker recovery workflows: user cannot quickly type a fix into the same terminal, change Node versions, accept a prompt, or stop a process without spending another agent turn. - No terminal command markers, shell integration readback, command reveal, or terminal-location telemetry equivalent unless we build another bridge.
Agent Host custom terminal path: what we gain and what it costs What we gain: - VS Code owns a PTY-backed terminal resource and can surface it in the terminal panel/editor. - Tool output can include a terminal URI/session ID, enabling Focus Terminal, command reveal, and terminal revival. - The agent can still get text output for the model while users can inspect the real terminal. - The agent can send input to a running shell via `write_*`, while users can also type directly into the terminal for sensitive prompts. - Long-running processes can remain visible and interruptible. - The implementation can share workbench terminal concepts: shell integration, command markers, history suppression, prompt/input detection, and terminal telemetry. What it costs: - Catching up to `runInTerminal` fidelity or more contained one with proper shell integration: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/317789 - Output shown to the model must stay reliable even when the visible terminal has control sequences, alternate screen, progress bars, or huge scrollback. - `write_*`/stdin paths require careful safety and prompt guidance, especially around secrets and permission prompts. - Sandbox behavior can diverge from the SDK shell path if not tested carefully. - There is maintenance cost if the SDK shell keeps evolving and VS Code overrides it.
Code references behind this - `chat.agentHost.customTerminalTool.enabled` is registered as an experimental/advanced setting that switches Copilot SDK sessions to the Agent Host terminal override. - `src/vs/platform/agentHost/common/agentService.ts` - `src/vs/platform/agentHost/common/agentHostCustomizationConfig.ts` - When the custom terminal tool is enabled, Agent Host builds shell tools that override SDK built-ins and are backed by `IAgentHostTerminalManager`. - `src/vs/platform/agentHost/node/copilot/copilotShellTools.ts` - The Agent Host custom tool currently provides primary shell execution plus `read_*`, `write_*`, shutdown, and list tools. The `write_*` path sends text to terminal stdin through `terminalManager.sendText(...)`. - `src/vs/platform/agentHost/node/copilot/copilotShellTools.ts` - Agent Host terminals inject VS Code shell integration, suppress shell history for agent commands, and set non-interactive env such as `PAGER=`, `GIT_PAGER=`, `GH_PAGER=`, and `GIT_TERMINAL_PROMPT=0`. - `src/vs/platform/agentHost/node/agentHostTerminalManager.ts` - The workbench/local agent terminal tool has first-class UX for user input and direct terminal interaction. - `src/vs/workbench/contrib/terminalContrib/chatAgentTools/browser/tools/runInTerminalTool.ts` - `src/vs/workbench/contrib/terminalContrib/chatAgentTools/browser/tools/sendToTerminalTool.ts` - `src/vs/workbench/contrib/terminalContrib/chatAgentTools/common/terminalSandboxService.ts` - The chat terminal UI can focus/reveal an existing terminal instance or open a terminal command URI. - `src/vs/workbench/contrib/chat/browser/widget/chatContentParts/toolInvocationParts/chatTerminalToolProgressPart.ts` - Tests already encode the important distinction: SDK built-in bash can render a terminal pill, but has no AHP terminal session/URI; terminal content blocks do set `terminalCommandUri`. - `src/vs/workbench/contrib/chat/test/browser/agentSessions/stateToProgressAdapter.test.ts` - Runtime context: Copilot CLI/runtime shell execution is now a direct child-process spawn path with stdio pipes rather than a PTY-backed terminal session. That path runs each command as a fresh child process, without terminal emulation, persistent PTY state, shell integration markers, or the same startup behavior as VS Code's integrated terminal. - Protocol context: AHP defines terminals as first-class subscribable resources with their own URIs, root-state listing, ownership claims, client-dispatchable input, server-dispatched output, resize/control actions, and optional command detection. - https://microsoft.github.io/agent-host-protocol/guide/terminals.html
**Workflow tests** Run each scenario three ways: - Local agent / workbench `runInTerminal` path where VS Code owns the terminal. - Agent Host with `chat.agentHost.customTerminalTool.enabled=true`. - Agent Host with `chat.agentHost.customTerminalTool.enabled=false`, relying on the SDK shell tool. For each test, record: Works / Partial / Missing, visible user behavior, model-visible output quality, accessibility notes, and whether the user can recover without another model turn. - [ ] **Baseline command execution**: one-shot command returns correct stdout, stderr, and exit code.
Manual test steps 1. Ask the agent to run: ```sh printf 'stdout-line\n'; printf 'stderr-line\n' >&2; exit 7 ``` 2. Expected with all paths: the agent can report stdout, stderr, and non-zero exit. 3. Extra signal for terminal-backed paths: the command is visible in a terminal UI and can be focused/revealed. 4. Extra signal for SDK shell path: output should be clean and parseable without requiring a terminal.
- [ ] **Persistent shell state**: a separate follow-up command can observe state set by a previous command.
Manual test steps 1. Ask the agent: "In the terminal, create `.tmp/agent-host-terminal-test/subdir`, cd into it, export `AHP_TERMINAL_STATE=kept`, and stop." 2. In a separate follow-up request, ask: "Without cd'ing again, run `pwd` and `printf '%s\n' "$AHP_TERMINAL_STATE"`." 3. Expected with a real persistent VS Code terminal: the second command sees the subdir cwd and env var. 4. Expected risk with SDK shell path: if execution is process-spawn based rather than a persistent shell, cwd/env changes from a previous child process may not survive unless the runtime models them separately. 5. Record whether the agent compensates by chaining commands or whether the terminal/session itself truly preserves state.
- [ ] **Python environment auto-activation**: agent shell execution uses the same Python environment a user would get from VS Code terminal activation. Related: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/323164
Manual test steps 1. In a Python workspace, create a local venv: ```sh python3 -m venv .tmp/agent-host-python-venv ``` 2. Select that interpreter in VS Code. 3. In a normal VS Code integrated terminal, verify the selected environment is auto-activated: ```sh python -c 'import os, sys; print(sys.executable); print(os.environ.get("VIRTUAL_ENV"))' ``` 4. Ask the agent to run the same command without explicitly activating the venv first. 5. Expected with local/workbench terminal: `python` resolves to the selected venv and/or `VIRTUAL_ENV` is set the same way a user terminal would be. 6. Expected with Agent Host custom terminal: terminal creation should preserve the VS Code/Python activation behavior, or at least expose the gap clearly. 7. Expected risk with SDK shell path: the shell command may run as a child process without VS Code/Python terminal activation scripts, so `python` may resolve to the system interpreter and `VIRTUAL_ENV` may be empty. 8. Record whether the agent notices the wrong interpreter before running tests/scripts, or whether it burns extra turns discovering the environment mismatch.
- [ ] **Interactive prompt**: the user or agent can answer a prompt in the same running process. - Side note: things like `sudo __` may take extra step or two: Not sure if this is something we can avoid by tweaking the prompt for the model now we are in non-pty world for CLI SDK built-in shell tool. Need to test other interactive workflow such as with installs and multiple prompts, etc
Manual test steps 1. Ask the agent to run: ```sh python3 -c 'name=input("Project name: "); print("created", name)' ``` 2. Expected with local/workbench terminal: the terminal shows the prompt; `sendToTerminal` or direct typing can answer it. 3. Expected with Agent Host custom terminal: `write_bash`/`write_powershell` can answer it, and the user should also be able to focus/type in the terminal. 4. Expected risk with SDK shell path: no VS Code terminal URI and no general stdin/write tool means the process may hang, time out, or require the agent to rerun with non-interactive flags. 5. Accessibility signal: screen reader users should be told the terminal is awaiting input and have a Focus Terminal action.
- [ ] **Sensitive prompt**: the user can type a secret directly into the terminal without sending it through the model.
Manual test steps 1. Ask the agent to run: ```sh python3 -c 'import getpass; v=getpass.getpass("Token: "); print("got-token", bool(v))' ``` 2. Type a dummy value such as `test-token` directly into the terminal. Do not send it through chat. 3. Expected with terminal-backed paths: Focus Terminal is available and the secret stays in terminal input. 4. Expected risk with SDK shell path: if there is no focused terminal/input channel, the agent may fail, ask in chat, or be unable to continue the original process.
- [ ] **Long-running command**: a slow command makes progress visible and can be focused while running.
Manual test steps 1. Ask the agent to run: ```sh python3 -u -c 'import time; [print(f"tick {i}") or time.sleep(1) for i in range(20)]' ``` 2. While it runs, verify whether the terminal panel/editor can be focused. 3. Expected with terminal-backed paths: user can see streaming output in a terminal, reveal the command, and interrupt if needed. 4. Expected with SDK shell path: the model may get partial output via `read_*`, but the user experience is inline text only unless VS Code adds a separate terminal-like surface.
- [ ] **Dev server / watcher**: a server/watch process can keep running while remaining visible and stoppable.
Manual test steps 1. In a temporary folder, ask the agent to run: ```sh python3 -m http.server 8765 ``` 2. Verify the user can see the live terminal output, focus the terminal, and stop it with Ctrl+C or the appropriate tool. 3. Then ask the agent to make a follow-up file/read command while the server remains running. 4. Expected with terminal-backed paths: the server remains visible in a terminal and user-controlled. 5. Expected with SDK shell path: background execution may be represented by a shell ID and `read_*`/`stop_*`, but not by a VS Code terminal the user can inspect or type into.
- [ ] **TTY-sensitive behavior**: we understand where non-TTY SDK execution helps and where it changes behavior.
Manual test steps 1. Ask the agent to run: ```sh python3 -c 'import sys, os; print("isatty", sys.stdout.isatty()); print("TERM", os.environ.get("TERM"))' ``` 2. Run a command likely to page or colorize in a terminal: ```sh git log --oneline --decorate -n 200 ``` 3. Expected with terminal-backed paths: commands may detect a TTY and use terminal behavior. 4. Expected with SDK shell path: commands likely see non-TTY/pipes, which can be better for parseable output but worse if the task depends on actual terminal behavior. 5. Decision question: do we need VS Code terminal fidelity, SDK non-TTY cleanliness, or both depending on command type?
- [ ] **REPL / TUI / alternate screen**: interactive programs are either supported or explicitly out of scope.
Manual test steps 1. Ask the agent to run: ```sh python3 -i -c 'print("ready")' ``` 2. Try entering `1 + 1`, then `exit()`. 3. Optional, if installed: try a harmless TUI such as `top`, `less package.json`, or an editor opening a scratch file. 4. Expected with terminal-backed paths: the user can interact directly with the running program. 5. Expected with SDK shell path: no PTY/terminal UI means REPL/TUI fidelity is missing or must be treated as unsupported.
- [ ] **Large output and scrollback**: users can inspect large output without losing terminal affordances.
Manual test steps 1. Ask the agent to run: ```sh python3 - <<'PY' for i in range(600): print(f"line {i:03d}") PY ``` 2. Expected with terminal-backed paths: user can scroll terminal output and reveal the command. 3. Expected with SDK shell path: output may be compacted, truncated, or written to a file/log for model consumption; verify whether the user can inspect the same output ergonomically. 4. Record whether inline output, terminal scrollback, and model-visible output agree.
- [ ] **Multiline commands, heredocs, and pasted blocks**: multiline input executes exactly once and preserves bytes.
Manual test steps 1. Ask the agent to run: ```sh mkdir -p .tmp/agent-host-terminal-test cat > .tmp/agent-host-terminal-test/heredoc.txt <<'EOF' one two with spaces $HOME should not expand EOF python3 - <<'PY' from pathlib import Path p = Path(".tmp/agent-host-terminal-test/heredoc.txt") print(repr(p.read_text())) PY ``` 2. Expected with workbench/local terminal: rich/basic/none execute strategies should send multiline commands safely. 3. Expected with Agent Host custom terminal: must preserve exact input and not duplicate/stale output. 4. Expected with SDK shell path: script-style execution may work well, but it does not prove visible terminal parity.
- [ ] **Hanging command / cancellation / recovery**: the user can tell what is happening and recover from a stuck process.
Manual test steps 1. Ask the agent to run: ```sh python3 -u -c 'import time; print("started"); time.sleep(120)' ``` 2. Try: focus terminal, press Ctrl+C, then run `echo recovered`. 3. Expected with terminal-backed paths: user can interrupt and continue in the same terminal. 4. Expected with SDK shell path: `stop_*` may kill the process, but the user cannot directly interact with the terminal because there is no VS Code terminal instance.
- [ ] **Terminal focus, reveal, hidden terminal, and telemetry**: the chat UI exposes an obvious way to inspect shell execution.
Manual test steps 1. Run any command that streams output for several seconds. 2. Collapse/hide the inline terminal output if possible. 3. Verify whether there is a Focus Terminal / Show Terminal action. 4. Verify whether the terminal opens in the expected location based on user settings. 5. Expected with terminal-backed paths: `terminalCommandUri` or terminal session ID allows focus/reveal and telemetry. 6. Expected with SDK shell path: tests currently expect no AHP terminal URI for built-in bash, so focus/reveal requires a different mechanism.
- [ ] **Session switch, reload, and terminal revival**: a terminal-backed command remains inspectable across chat/session transitions.
Manual test steps 1. Start a long-running command or dev server. 2. Switch away from the chat session and return. 3. Reload the window if supported by the scenario. 4. Expected with Agent Host terminal content blocks: terminal URI/session data can revive or reopen the terminal-backed view. 5. Expected with SDK shell path: verify what persists beyond textual task state and whether the user can inspect the original process/output.
- [ ] **Default shell, profile, and environment parity**: agent shell behavior matches VS Code terminal expectations.
Manual test steps 1. Configure a non-default integrated terminal profile, or `terminal.integrated.agentHostProfile.` if applicable. 2. Ask the agent to run: ```sh printf 'SHELL=%s\n' "$SHELL"; printf 'PATH=%s\n' "$PATH"; printf 'TERM=%s\n' "$TERM" ``` 3. Expected with local/workbench terminal: behavior follows the VS Code terminal profile. 4. Expected with Agent Host custom terminal: verify the pushed shell path is honored; note that current config describes only the shell executable path, not profile args/env. 5. Expected with SDK shell path: verify whether SDK shell configuration matches VS Code user expectations or chooses its own shell/process flags.
- [ ] **Accessibility-specific prompt flow**: screen reader users can understand that input is needed and act without guessing.
Manual test steps 1. With screen reader mode enabled, run the ordinary input prompt scenario. 2. Verify announcement/accessible view behavior for "terminal is awaiting input." 3. Verify the user can focus the terminal and type directly. 4. Expected with terminal-backed paths: terminal UI and chat elicitation/focus affordance should make the input state clear. 5. Expected with SDK shell path: decide what equivalent accessibility surface exists if no VS Code terminal is attached.
**Decision checklist** - [ ] Complete the workflow matrix above for local/workbench terminal, Agent Host custom terminal, and SDK shell path. - [ ] Decide whether missing terminal visibility/input is acceptable product behavior or a blocker for VS Code Agent Host. - [ ] Identify which failures are resolvable via additional support from SDK. - [ ] Decide whether `chat.agentHost.customTerminalTool.enabled` should remain experimental, become the supported/default path, or be replaced by a smaller SDK terminal integration API. - [ ] Define minimum parity before disabling/removing the custom path: Focus Terminal, direct user input, prompt/accessibility behavior, long-running command visibility, cancellation/recovery, and terminal telemetry. - [ ] If we keep the SDK shell default, define explicit UX for "no real terminal exists" so users do not think VS Code lost terminal support accidentally. VS Code's terminal value is broader than command execution: users expect visible output, direct intervention, accessibility affordances, terminal scrollback, command reveal, profile/shell fidelity, and a way to recover when the agent gets it wrong. This issue tracks whether Agent Host needs a VS Code-owned custom terminal tool to preserve those workflows, and what must be true if we rely on the SDK shell tool instead.

title: Agent host: client tool calls in the default chat hang (never resolved)
body:
## Problem

Client-provided tool calls running in a session's **default chat** can hang: the SDK handler's deferred is never resolved, so the agent waits forever on a tool result that already came back.

## Root cause

`ChatToolCallComplete` envelopes are emitted on the chat channel URI, which is formatted as:

```
ahp-chat://<chatId>/<base64(sessionUri)>
```

In `agentSideEffects.ts`, both dispatch paths forwarded that chat URI straight into `IAgent.onClientToolCallComplete(session, ...)`. But every agent keys its `_sessions` map by the raw session id, resolved via `AgentSession.id(uri)` = `uri.path.substring(1)`. For a chat URI that path is the **base64 blob**, not the session GUID, so the default-chat lookup never matched and the completion was silently dropped.

This affected the copilot, claude, and codex agents. Peer (additional, non-default) chats happened to still work because they resolve through a separate `_chatSessions` map keyed by the chat URI.

## Fix

Forward the resolved session URI for the default chat (so `AgentSession.id` yields the GUID), and keep forwarding the chat URI for peer chats. Tracked in [#323302](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/pull/323302).

## Follow-ups to consider

- The footgun is that `onClientToolCallComplete(session, ...)` accepts a URI that is sometimes a session URI and sometimes a chat URI, and each agent re-derives routing. Worth considering a clearer contract or a single shared resolution helper so this can't silently regress.
- Add broader coverage that the URI handed to agents at the side-effects boundary is always the one the agent can resolve.

title: Copilot session restore splits one interaction into multiple chat responses
body:
## Type of Bug

Bug

## Description

Restoring an Agent Host Copilot session can split one SDK interaction into multiple AHP turns and therefore multiple `ChatResponseViewModel` instances. The additional requests have empty prompts.

The Copilot SDK can persist empty `assistant.message` events with no tool requests immediately before another assistant message in the same `interactionId` and SDK `turnId`. The live adapter ignores these non-renderable messages, but `mapSessionEvents` treats every parent assistant message without tool requests as the end of the current turn. The following assistant message then has no active builder, so history replay creates a fallback turn with an empty user message.

The resulting chain is:

1. `mapSessionEvents` prematurely finalizes the parent turn.
2. The next assistant message creates a fallback turn with an empty prompt.
3. `turnsToHistory` emits a request/response pair for every reconstructed turn.
4. `ChatServiceImpl` and `ChatViewModel` create multiple top-level response view models.

Captured restored models showed six empty requests created within the same millisecond. Their request IDs exactly matched the orphaned SDK `assistant.message` event IDs.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Run a tool-heavy Copilot Agent Host conversation that produces multiple SDK model rounds.
2. Switch away from the session or restart/reopen it so history is rebuilt from SDK events.
3. Inspect the chat model or rendered transcript.
4. Observe multiple requests/responses with empty prompts for what was one live interaction.

## Expected Behavior

All assistant messages belonging to the same SDK interaction are reconstructed into one AHP turn and one top-level chat response, preserving markdown/tool ordering. Empty, non-renderable assistant events must not create a turn boundary.

## Relevant Code

- `src/vs/platform/agentHost/node/copilot/mapSessionEvents.ts`
- `src/vs/platform/agentHost/test/node/mapSessionEvents.test.ts`

(Written by Copilot)

title: Agent Host client tool hangs after active client removal; browser click invoked with empty args
body:
## Summary

A browser `clickElement` tool call can get stuck in `Preparing` after the Agent Host removes the active client. The click never reaches Playwright; the workbench invokes the browser tool with `{}` parameters and crashes during tool preparation.

## Observed

In an Agents/Copilot CLI session using built-in browser tools:

1. `openBrowserPage` succeeds.
2. `clickElement` on the initial `Start` button succeeds.
3. `screenshotPage` succeeds.
4. The next `clickElement` on the board's `Run` button stays stuck at `Preparing`.

The session event stream ends with:

```text
tool.execution_start clickElement
permission.requested clickElement
# no permission.completed
# no tool.execution_complete
```

## Key log evidence

The model requested valid arguments:

```json
{
  "element": "Run button on Research Agent",
  "pageId": "<page-id>",
  "ref": "e200"
}
```

But the workbench invoked the underlying browser tool with empty parameters:

```text
[AgentHost] Invoking client tool: clickElement
[LanguageModelToolsService#invokeTool] Invoking tool click_element with parameters {}
[error] Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'toString')
    at createBrowserPageLink(...)
    at ClickBrowserTool.prepareToolInvocation(...)
```

The AHP sequence for the same tool call was:

```text
chat/toolCallStart clickElement
chat/toolCallReady confirmed="not-needed"           # no toolInput
chat/toolCallComplete success=false error="Client <client-id> disconnected before completing clickElement"
chat/toolCallReady confirmation UI with real toolInput
```

About 6 seconds earlier, the same session received:

```text
session/activeClientRemoved clientId=<client-id>
```

## Suspected cause

This looks like an Agent Host/client-tool lifecycle race:

- The active client is removed from session state while the UI/client connection is still participating in the turn.
- A later client-contributed browser tool call is treated as orphaned/disconnected.
- The synthetic `toolCallReady` without `toolInput` is consumed by the workbench client-tool handler.
- The handler falls back to `{}` and invokes `click_element` before the later `toolCallReady` containing the real `toolInput` arrives.
- `ClickBrowserTool.prepareToolInvocation` assumes `pageId` is present, calls `createBrowserPageLink(undefined)`, throws, and the original Copilot tool execution remains stuck waiting for completion/permission state.

Relevant code paths:

- `src/vs/platform/agentHost/node/protocolServerHandler.ts`
  - disconnected client tool call handling emits synthetic ready/complete events
- `src/vs/workbench/contrib/chat/browser/agentSessions/agentHost/agentHostSessionHandler.ts`
  - missing `toolInput` falls back to `{}` once status is not `Streaming`
- `src/vs/workbench/contrib/browserView/electron-browser/tools/clickBrowserTool.ts`
  - `prepareToolInvocation` assumes `pageId` is defined

## Expected

A disconnected/orphaned client-tool call should fail cleanly and unblock the model/UI, or be retried with a replacement active client. It should not invoke the local browser tool with `{}` parameters or leave the tool call stuck in `Preparing`.

## Possible fixes

- Do not invoke client tools from `toolCallReady` events that lack `toolInput` when the tool has required arguments or `_meta.toolArguments` exists.
- Ensure disconnected-client synthetic `toolCallReady`/`toolCallComplete` ordering cannot race with later confirmation UI events for the same tool call.
- Harden browser tool `prepareToolInvocation` against missing `pageId` so malformed protocol state becomes a clean tool error.
- Investigate why `session/activeClientRemoved` fired while the client was still successfully confirming/completing tool calls in the same session.

title: [AHP/CLI:COGS] Tools Grouping Service (Virtual Tools)
body:
**Priority:** P0

The Tools Grouping Service ("virtual tools"), built by @connor4312, keeps large tool sets from inflating prompt size and degrading tool selection. Once the active tool count crosses a threshold, related tools are collapsed into onΓÇædemand **virtual tool groups** so the model sees a compact set instead of a long flat list ΓÇö a COGS win that grows with the number of MCP/extension tools. We need this behavior to carry over as CLI/AHP becomes the default agent.

## How it works

- Grouping kicks in once the tool count passes `START_GROUPING_AFTER_TOOL_COUNT` (currently `HARD_TOOL_LIMIT / 2 = 64`), keeping the request under the 128 hard tool limit. The threshold is experimentΓÇæbased (`VirtualToolThreshold`).
- `IToolGroupingService.create(sessionId, tools)` returns an `IToolGrouping` that:
  - `compute(query)` ΓÇö returns the (possibly grouped) tool list for the request;
  - expands a group on demand when the model calls into it (`didCall` / `ensureExpanded`), and collapses tools by recency across turns (`didTakeTurn`);
  - is backed by a persisted grouping cache (`ToolGroupingCache`).
- Categorization uses the clientΓÇæside embeddings service to cluster related tools.

Lives in the Copilot extension under `extension/tools/common/virtualTools/` (`toolGroupingService.ts`, `toolGrouping.ts`, `virtualToolGrouper.ts`, `virtualToolGroupCache.ts`).

## Why it needs attention for AHP/CLI

- The service runs in the Copilot extension and depends on the clientΓÇæside embeddings service, so it does **not** automatically apply to CLI / agentΓÇæhost (SDK) sessions.
- As with Client Side Tool Search, we need SDK/CLI buyΓÇæin to let the client supply tool grouping ΓÇö or an equivalent path ΓÇö so the token savings hold when CLI is the default. The client would keep the embeddings index updated against the SDK tool set.

## References

- Umbrella: #321462 ΓÇö Client Side Tool Search & Tools Grouping Service
- Original implementation by @connor4312 (virtual tools: initial implementation, autoΓÇætrigger at the 128 tool limit, enablement simplification, lifecycle fixes).

---
**Context:** Part of the CLI/AHPΓÇæasΓÇædefault migration. Tracking given the COGS impact of tool grouping on large tool sets.

title: [AHP/CLI] Claude Opus 4.7 / 4.8 Alt Prompt
body:
Tracking the experimental, versionΓÇæspecific alternate system prompts for Claude Opus 4.7 and 4.8. Both are optΓÇæin (default off) and layer modelΓÇæspecific tuning on top of the base prompt, following Anthropic's perΓÇæversion prompting guides.

## Opus 4.7 ΓÇö Copilot extension

A standalone `Claude47OpusPrompt` (copy of `Claude46OpusPrompt`) gated by the experimentΓÇæbased setting `github.copilot.chat.claude47OpusPrompt.enabled` (default `false`). When enabled, `claude-opus-4.7*` traffic routes to it; otherwise it falls back to the 4.6 Opus prompt.

Highlights vs 4.6 (per the Opus 4.7 prompting guide): refined toolΓÇætriggering and subagent fanΓÇæout, responseΓÇæshape/verbosity wording, full operationalSafety, plus targeted lessons ΓÇö no internal narration between tool calls, endΓÇæofΓÇæturn summary cap, comment discipline, "don't duplicate inΓÇæflight subagent work", "verify subagent output before reporting done", and a `<skillUsage>` primer.

- PR: #313916 (cherryΓÇæpick #314193)

## Opus 4.8 ΓÇö Agent host (CLI)

Delivered through the new perΓÇæmodel systemΓÇæprompt registry for Copilot agentΓÇæhost sessions. `Claude48OpusPromptResolver` matches Opus 4.8 and is gated by the VS Code setting `chat.agentHost.opus48Prompt.enabled` (agentΓÇæhost config `opus48Prompt`, default off). When off it returns `undefined` and the session uses the default SDK system message.

Per Anthropic's Opus 4.8 guide the overrides are intentionally minimal ΓÇö the model performs well out of the box ΓÇö keeping the SDK foundation (tool/safety sections) intact and only nudging two behaviors:
- **Tone:** concise, focused responses; minimal examples; sparing emoji.
- **Subagents:** don't spawn for work doable in a single response; fan out multiple subagents in one turn when reading multiple files / iterating across items.

No interimΓÇæprogress scaffolding is added (the guide warns against forcing "summarize after every N tool calls").

- PR: #321864

## Reference

Opus 4.7 tracking, eval runs, and experiment: microsoft/vscode-internalbacklog#7600

title: Support Multi-Chat in Claude harness
body:
Summary

Add Multi-Chat support to the Claude harness so it matches the Copilot experience.

Background

- The Multi-Chat feature (previously called Sub-Sessions) is currently available for the Copilot harness and works in AHP sessions for Copilot.
- Team feedback and user studies have shown confusion around the feature and its name; the UX should be consistent across harnesses rather than Copilot-only.
- Claude AHP sessions do not currently show the Multi-Chat UI.

Proposal

- Implement Multi-Chat in the Claude harness with parity to the Copilot implementation and UX.
- Ensure Multi-Chat is available in AHP sessions for Claude as it is for Copilot.
- Minimize agent integration effort (sessions should be transparent to agents where possible), and document any agent-facing changes required.
- Update tests, e2e scenarios, and relevant documentation to reflect Multi-Chat availability in the Claude harness.

Acceptance criteria

- Multi-Chat UI appears and behaves the same in the Claude harness as it does in Copilot.
- Multi-Chat is available and functional in AHP sessions for Claude.
- Agent/handler code for Claude either requires no changes or any required changes are documented and minimal.
- Relevant tests and documentation updated.

Implementation notes

- Verify whether sessions need to be linked or if the agent surface can remain unchanged (transparent sessions).
- Coordinate UX changes with the Copilot harness team to ensure consistency and avoid repeating prior confusion around naming/behavior.
- Add testing steps to validate in manual and automated tests across the Claude harness and AHP sessions.

---

Split from the combined Claude & Codex issue. Codex tracked separately in #323624.

title: [AHP/CLI:COGS] Anthropic Extended Cache (1hr TTL)
body:
**Priority:** P0

Anthropic supports an extended **1ΓÇæhour cache TTL** for prompt caching, versus the default 5ΓÇæminute TTL. LongerΓÇælived cache entries can raise cacheΓÇæhit rates across longer agent sessions ΓÇö and across summarization/compaction boundaries ΓÇö which reduces the uncached input billed per turn. The catch is that 1h cache **writes** cost more, so the net COGS impact depends on how much reuse each workload actually gets.

## The tradeoff

- **5ΓÇæminute TTL (default):** cache write = **1.25├ù** base input.
- **1ΓÇæhour TTL (extended):** cache write = **2.0├ù** base input.
- Applies only to Anthropic extendedΓÇæTTLΓÇæeligible models (per `modelSupportsExtendedCacheTtl`); all other models are unaffected.

For COGS modeling, the extendedΓÇæTTL path prices uncached input at **base ├ù (2.0 / 1.25) = ├ù1.6** for eligible Anthropic models, so the write premium is reflected when comparing against the 5ΓÇæminute baseline. The win is only realized when the higher cacheΓÇæhit lift outweighs that 1.6├ù write premium.

## Open questions / followΓÇæups

- **Telemetry gap:** Anthropic returns `cache_creation.ephemeral_5m_input_tokens` and `ephemeral_1h_input_tokens` separately, but we only capture the combined total. A hybridΓÇæTTL strategy (e.g. system + tools on 1h, messages on 5m) can't be validated without a telemetry change to split these.
- Identify which models/workloads recoup the 1.6├ù write premium through higher cache reuse, and whether a hybridΓÇæTTL allocation is worth pursuing.

## Reference

Detailed COGS accounting and perΓÇæmodel breakdown: microsoft/vscode-internalbacklog#7825

---
**Context:** Part of the CLI/AHPΓÇæasΓÇædefault migration. Tracking given the direct COGS impact of cacheΓÇæTTL selection.

title: [AHP/CLI:COGS] Grep Search Tool Output Optimization
body:
The agent `grep_search` (`findTextInFiles`) tool was returning verbose, XMLΓÇætagged results that dominated its toolΓÇæcall token usage. PRs #321601 and #321938 introduce a compact, grepΓÇæstyle output format behind an experiment. Early results show the new format roughly **halves** the tokens returned by the tool with no loss of useful signal ΓÇö a direct COGS win as we move CLI/AHP to the default agent.

## What changed

- **New compact output format** ΓÇö A `grep`-style result (plain `path` followed by `line:text`, blankΓÇæline separated) replaces the previous tagΓÇæwrapped output. Gated by the experimentΓÇæbased setting `github.copilot.chat.tools.grepSearch.outputFormat` (`tag` | `grep`, default `tag`).
  - #321601 ΓÇö Optimize grep output and enhance configuration
- **TokenΓÇæbudgetΓÇæaware rendering + longΓÇæline handling** ΓÇö Results render through promptΓÇætsx, so they participate in tokenΓÇæbudget pruning (earlier files prioritized) and carry editor references instead of a single unprunable blob. Long lines are truncated to a max length with context kept around the match.
  - #321938 ΓÇö Fix long line rendering for grep style output
- **Smarter result capping** ΓÇö The `maxResults` budget is distributed proportionally across matching files (largestΓÇæremainder, min one match per file), and paths are returned workspaceΓÇærelative.

## Experiment

[`agent.tool.responselength`](https://exp.microsoft.com/features/30687fdb-eb7c-dbe8-06c4-02c5ece0671a/progressions?workspaceId=dbd12384-5001-4e90-a863-526321eaf233) ΓÇö treatment enables `grepSearch.outputFormat = grep` via the `github.copilot-chat/agent.tool.responselength` flag.

### Tokens returned by `grep_search` (control = `tag`, treatment = `grep`)

| Group | Events | Total Tokens | Avg | P50 | P95 | P99 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| control | 363,400 | 421,529,887 | 1,160 | 321.2 | 5,275.4 | 13,091.6 |
| treatment | 389,097 | 200,998,321 | 516.6 | 137.7 | 2,123.6 | 6,380.8 |

- **~55% fewer tokens per call** on average (1,160 ΓåÆ 517); P95 down ~60%, P99 down ~51%.
- Total tokens dropped from **421.5M ΓåÆ 201.0M**, despite treatment serving *more* calls (389K vs 363K events).
- `PValue = 0` ΓÇö statistically significant.

---
**Context:** Part of the CLI/AHPΓÇæasΓÇædefault migration. Tracking given the direct COGS impact of tool response length.

title: Copilot CLI agent (Agents window) gets stuck reading files via GitHub MCP instead of the local worktree
body:
## Description

When using the **Copilot CLI agent inside the Agents window** (with a local git worktree open), the agent frequently gets stuck. Instead of reading files directly from my **local filesystem**, it repeatedly falls back to **GitHub commands/tools** (e.g. `get_file_contents`, code search against the remote repo) to read source files that already exist locally.

This causes the agent to:
- Loop for a long time fetching files remotely that are sitting right there on disk
- Burn through premium/usage cost ("dollars") without making progress
- Effectively stall for long stretches before producing any useful output

## Steps to reproduce

1. Open VS Code with a local git **worktree** of a repository.
2. Open the **Agents** window and start a **Copilot CLI** agent session.
3. Ask the agent to perform a task that requires reading several local files.
4. Observe the agent choosing GitHub remote file-reading tools instead of local file reads.

## Expected behavior

- The agent should **prefer local file reads** (the open worktree on disk) over GitHub remote fetches whenever the files are available locally.
- Remote GitHub tools should only be used when the content genuinely is not available locally.
- The agent should not get stuck in long loops re-fetching files remotely.

## Actual behavior

- The agent uses GitHub commands to read files that already exist in the local worktree.
- It gets stuck "analysing for hours," consuming usage/cost with no progress.

## Impact

- Wasted time (sessions stall for long periods).
- Wasted money ΓÇö usage is consumed without delivering results.

## Environment

- Surface: VS Code **Agents** window, **Copilot CLI** agent
- Working directory: local git **worktree**

## Additional notes

A clear local-first file-access policy (or stronger tool-selection guidance that deprioritizes remote GitHub reads when a local workspace/worktree is present) would likely resolve this.

title: Chat: system notification XML tags rendered raw in conversation
body: ## Problem When the Copilot agent runtime sends system notifications (e.g., background agent completion), the `` XML tags are rendered as raw text in the chat conversation instead of being hidden or styled appropriately. ## Expected behavior System notifications wrapped in `...` tags should either be: - Hidden from the visible conversation (they are metadata for the model, not the user) - Or rendered as a styled, dismissible notification element ## Actual behavior The raw XML tags appear verbatim in the chat output, e.g.: ``` Background agent explore-session-artifacts completed. ``` ## Context These notifications are injected by the agent runtime as non-billable system messages to inform the model about async events (shell command completions, background agent results). They are not intended to be user-facing content.

title: [AHP/CLI: COGS] Compaction Improvements
body:
**Priority:** P2

Our approach adds transcripts post-compaction for reference and lookup; CLI uses checkpoints instead. Transcripts allow models to reference previous tool calls and prevent token-wasteful loops after compaction.

Also ensure that we always have a warm cache before we kick off compaction (even at a new turn boundaries).

title: [AHP:COGS]: Cache Checkpoint Gap
body:
**Priority:** P3

Our implementation uses one additional cache checkpoint compared to the SDK (which has 3). This extra checkpoint is good to have.

title: [AHP/CLI:COGS] Subagent Scenarios
body:
**Priority:** P2

Execution, Search, and Summarization subagents are ready to ship in VSCode; need to be ported to the new harness.

---
**Context:** We're migrating to using the CLI in AHP as the default. Tracking this item given the COGS impact.

title: [AHP/CLI:COGS] Token Consumption & Prompt Optimization
body:
**Priority:** P1

Opus48 on CLI uses 200k more tokens per turn than VSCode chat. 
Additional insight: VSCode users average fewer turns per session than CLI users, so there may be system prompt differences which might affect our workloads. Plan to review and run evals.

---
**Context:** We're migrating to using the CLI in AHP as the default. Tracking this item given the COGS impact.

title: [AHP/CLI:COGS] Client Side Tool Search & Tools Grouping Service
body:
**Priority:** P0

Upstream issue:https://github.com/github/copilot-mcp-core/issues/1818 ΓÇö Bridging the gap between VS Code and Copilot CLI/SDK tool search

## Model support

Both Anthropic and OpenAI natively support tool search, surfaced through the `supports.tool_search` capability flag on `IChatModelCapabilities`:

- [`extensions/copilot/src/platform/endpoint/common/endpointProvider.ts` ΓåÆ `IChatModelCapabilities`](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/main/extensions/copilot/src/platform/endpoint/common/endpointProvider.ts#L33-L60) (see `supports.tool_search`)

## SDK-side buy-in to allow client-supplied tool search

Our tool search tool is complex and depends on the client-side embeddings service, so we've gotten buy-in from the SDK / CLI folks to let clients pass in their own tool search rather than relying solely on the built-in one.

On our side, the client will update our embeddings service to also index on the SDK tools, keeping client-side tool search accurate as the SDK tool set evolves.

---
**Context:** We're migrating to using the CLI in AHP as the default. Tracking this item given the COGS impact.

title: Subagents sometimes show up in session list
Missing changesets on AH session
Copilot Agent Host: SDK Driven Agents/Skills/etc
Add /chronicle command set to Agent Host
body:
## Summary

Add the `/chronicle` command family (standup, search, tips, cost-tips, improve, reindex) to the Agent Host so users can invoke session history insights directly from VS Code chat.

## Motivation

The `/chronicle` commands provide powerful session history analysis (standup reports, usage tips, cost optimization, workflow improvements) via the `session_store_sql` tool. These are currently available through the CLI but not surfaced in VS Code's Agent Host. Users should be able to access these from the same chat surface where they do their work.

## Subcommands

| Subcommand | Behavior | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `standup` | agent-prompt | Get a report on your work from the last day |
| `search <query>` | agent-prompt | Search all session content by keyword or topic |
| `tips` | agent-prompt | Get personalized tips based on usage patterns |
| `cost-tips` | agent-prompt | Get tips to reduce token usage and cost |
| `improve` | agent-prompt | Suggest improvements to copilot-instructions.md |
| `reindex` | local (no model) | Rebuild the session store index |

## Implementation Plan

### Phase 1: Completion Provider (discoverability)

1. **Extend `CopilotSlashCommandName`** in `copilotSlashCommandCompletionProvider.ts` ΓÇö add `'chronicle'` to the type union, `COMMANDS` array, and `getCommandDescription()`. Insert text should be `/chronicle ` (trailing space to prompt subcommand entry).

2. **Add chronicle subcommand completions** ΓÇö detect `/chronicle ` prefix and offer sub-completions for the 6 subcommands. Either extend the existing provider or create a dedicated provider.

3. **Update `parseLeadingSlashCommand()` regex** to recognize `/chronicle` followed by optional subcommand + remaining text.

### Phase 2: Command Execution (send handler)

4. **Add chronicle dispatch in `CopilotAgentSession.send()`** ΓÇö parse subcommand from `slashCommand.rest`, build the agent prompt, inject as message text. For `reindex`: complete turn locally (like `/compact`).

5. **Create `chroniclePrompts.ts`** ΓÇö new file at `src/vs/platform/agentHost/node/copilot/chroniclePrompts.ts` with prompt builders for each subcommand (standup, search, tips, cost-tips, improve). Each prompt instructs the model to use `session_store_sql` with the appropriate schema.

### Phase 3: Tool & Store Availability

6. **Ensure `session_store_sql` is available** ΓÇö verify the tool is registered for agent host sessions when chronicle prompts are injected. The tool needs access to the session store (cloud DuckDB or local SQLite).

### Phase 4: Feature Gating

7. **Add config/feature flag** ΓÇö gate behind a config key (similar to `AgentHostConfigKey.RubberDuck`). Hide from completions when disabled or when no session store is available.

## Relevant Files

- `src/vs/platform/agentHost/node/copilot/copilotSlashCommandCompletionProvider.ts` ΓÇö command list, type, regex
- `src/vs/platform/agentHost/node/copilot/copilotAgentSession.ts` ΓÇö `send()` dispatch
- `src/vs/platform/agentHost/node/copilot/chroniclePrompts.ts` ΓÇö **new**, prompt builders
- `src/vs/platform/agentHost/node/copilot/copilotAgent.ts` ΓÇö provider registration
- `extensions/copilot/src/extension/tools/common/toolNames.ts` ΓÇö already has `SessionStoreSql`
- `extensions/copilot/src/extension/chronicle/vscode-node/sessionStoreTracker.ts` ΓÇö session store writer (reference)

## Design Decisions

- **Prompt injection pattern**: Same approach as `/plan`, `/research`, `/rubber-duck` ΓÇö rewrite prompt text in `send()` before forwarding to the SDK.
- **Cloud vs. local store**: Target cloud session store (DuckDB syntax) since the agent host is connected to GitHub. Fall back to local SQLite if cloud is unavailable.
- **Subcommand completion UX**: Space-separated form (`/chronicle standup`) matching existing parser behavior.
- **`reindex` handling**: Complete turn locally (like `/compact`) since it's a side-effect, not a model interaction.
- **`/chronicle search` without query**: Return inline help text rather than invoking the model.

## Verification

1. Type `/` ΓåÆ chronicle appears in completion list with description
2. Type `/chronicle ` ΓåÆ subcommand completions appear
3. Submit `/chronicle standup` ΓåÆ model receives prompt and uses `session_store_sql`
4. Submit `/chronicle reindex` ΓåÆ completes locally without model
5. Feature flag off ΓåÆ command hidden from completions and rejected in send
6. Unit tests for command parsing and prompt generation

title: Agent host: "Trying to add a disposable to a DisposableStore that has already been disposed of" from in-process node.js file watcher
body:
Seeing this error in the Main process output today, I think it shows when the window starts. Copilot flagged https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/pull/320038 as related cc @DonJayamanne. I think it's harmless but just cleaning it up by fixing the disposable tracking. I'm just wondering whether the watcher churn can be reduced, I don't know anything about the area though.

Copilot text below:

## Problem

The agent host logs the following warning repeatedly:

```
Error: Trying to add a disposable to a DisposableStore that has already been disposed of. The added object will be leaked!
    at r.add (.../agentHostMain.js)
    at r._register (.../agentHostMain.js)
    at r.watch (.../agentHostMain.js)
    at process.processTicksAndRejections (node:internal/process/task_queues:104:5)
```

## Root cause

In `NodeJSFileWatcherLibrary.watch()` (`src/vs/platform/files/node/watcher/nodejs/nodejsWatcherLib.ts`):

```ts
this._register(await this.doWatch(stat.isDirectory()));
```

`doWatch()` is async. If the watcher instance is disposed while that promise is in flight, `_store` is already disposed by the time `this._register(...)`  producing the warning and leaking the underlying `fs.watch` handle.runs 

## Why it shows up "a lot" in the agent host

- The agent host runs the non-recursive node.js watcher **in-process** (`NodeJSWatcherClient` instantiates `NodeJSWatcher` directly inside `agentHostMain.js`), so the warning surfaces directly in agent host logs instead of a separate watcher process.
- `SessionCustomizationDiscovery` watches well-known customization roots (workspace root, `.github`, `.claude`, `.copilot`, user home, agents/skills dirs) non-recursively and, on any relevant file change, tears down all those watchers and recreates them on the same paths (debounced). During an active session, files in the workspace root change frequently, so watchers are disposed and recreated  exposing the race above.rapidly 

The watcher churn itself is by design (rescans pick up directories that appear later). The warning + leaked handle is the actual bug.

## Fix

Use the existing `thenRegisterOrDispose` helper so the resolved watch handle is disposed (instead of registered and leaked) when the watcher was disposed during the async `doWatch()`.

## Possible follow-up (optional)

`SessionCustomizationDiscovery.refresh()` disposes *all* watchers on every relevant change and rebuilds them, even for roots that didn't change. Diffing the root set would reduce the churn.

title: Agent Feedback Feature for AHP for Agents Window
body:
### Overview
We propose to implement a commenting feature in the agents window that integrates with the agent host protocol. This feature will enhance collaboration and communication between users and agents by allowing users to comment on selected code snippets.

### Requirements
- **User Interaction**: Users should be able to select code, write comments about it, and submit these comments to the agent for further action.
- **Review Agent Functionality**: A designated review agent should have the ability to create comments that users can view, review, delete, and respond to. This will facilitate a discussion around the code.
- **Agent Collaboration**: The system should allow one agent to create comments while another agent tackles them, promoting an orchestrated workflow.
- **Server-Side State Management**: The state of comments should be maintained server-side to ensure functionality in remote AHP setups, where no client is connected. This will allow users to see comments across different clients and devices.

### Golden Scenario

A typical end-to-end scenario would look like this:

1. A user implements a feature in an initial chat (Chat 1).
2. The user starts a review agent in a separate chat, but within the same session.
3. The review agent analyses the code and adds comments independently, potentially continuing its work even while the client is disconnected from the server.
4. When the user returns, they can:
  - Browse all generated comments
  - Discard comments they donΓÇÖt find relevant
  - Instruct an agent to address selected comments
5. Another agent (or the same one) can then tackle the feedback, creating an iterative workflow across chats.

This scenario enables asynchronous, multi-agent collaboration while keeping the user in control of what feedback is applied.

title: Support Agent window access to WSL workspaces and terminal execution
body:
### Feature request

VS Code's Agent window should be able to operate directly against WSL-based workspaces when the editor is connected through Remote - WSL.

### Why this matters

A large part of my workflow runs inside WSL on Windows. Today, the Agent window can feel limited to the Windows host context, which creates friction when I want it to read files, run commands, or inspect a project that actually lives in WSL.

### Expected behavior

- The Agent window recognizes when the active workspace is in WSL.
- File reads and edits work directly on the WSL filesystem.
- Command execution runs in the WSL shell, not on the Windows host shell.
- Path handling is transparent and consistent for WSL paths.
- The experience feels as seamless as local non-WSL workspaces.

### Example use cases

- Open a Linux project in Remote - WSL and ask the Agent to run tests or start a server.
- Inspect and edit files inside a WSL workspace without manual path translation.
- Use the Agent window without switching back and forth between Windows and WSL terminals.

### Related note

I found a related closed issue about agent plugin hooks failing in WSL due to Windows-style path handling, but this request is broader: I want first-class Agent window support for WSL workspaces and execution, not just a hook-path fix.

title: Agent host CLI: kill_running_server leaks server node child process after upgrade
body:
## Summary

The CLI agent host supervisor's `kill_running_server` path leaks the underlying server node process when restarting after a `_vscodeUpgrade` RPC. The bash launcher script (`<server>/bin/code-server-<quality>`) is terminated, but the `node ... server-main.js` child it `spawn`'d remains alive as an orphan.

## Repro

Discovered while testing https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/pull/319455 against a remote macOS host using a pinned-old-server CLI:

1. Bring up an agent host supervisor on commit A (e.g. `7090241a056cb49cfe90f58da9a8cc44df9262a9`, protocol 0.2.0).
2. Connect a desktop on commit B (newer protocol 0.3.0). Handshake fails with `UnsupportedProtocolVersion`.
3. Click "Update Server". The supervisor receives the `_vscodeUpgrade` RPC, calls `kill_running_server`, then restarts on commit B.
4. Observe processes on the remote: the bash wrapper for commit A is gone, but its node child (e.g. `~/.<serverDataFolderName>/cli/servers/Insiders-7090241a.../server/node ... server-main.js`) is still alive with `ppid=1`.

In my test run the orphan was pid 230 and survived indefinitely; only the new commit-B server's child was tracked by the supervisor after the restart.

## Suspected cause

`cli/src/tunnels/agent_host.rs::kill_running_server` (or the platform-specific kill helper it delegates to) appears to signal the immediate child only. The server launcher is:

```
<server>/bin/code-server-<quality>      (bash)
  ΓööΓöÇΓöÇ node ... out/vs/server/node/server-main.js   (the server)
```

Bash does not forward `SIGTERM` to its children by default, and we don't wait on it before terminating the new generation, so the node child is reparented to PID 1 and continues running. It does eventually exit when its IPC pipe closes, but in our test it persisted for the duration of the run.

## Suggested fix

A couple of options, in rough order of preference:

1. **Signal the whole process group / job** from the CLI side ΓÇö i.e. set up the server child in its own process group at spawn time (`setsid` / `CREATE_NEW_PROCESS_GROUP`) and send `SIGTERM` to the negative pgid on shutdown. Robust regardless of what the launch script does.
2. **Make the launcher forward signals**, e.g. `trap` `SIGTERM` / `SIGINT` in `code-server-<quality>` and re-signal `$!` before `wait`. Cheaper change but doesn't help non-shell launchers.
3. **Replace the bash launcher with a direct exec** so the CLI's child *is* the node server. Simplest of all if there's no other reason to keep the shell shim.

## Scope

- CLI only (`cli/src/tunnels/agent_host.rs` and the server-bin shim at `<server>/bin/code-server-<quality>`).
- Independent of https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/pull/319455; that PR fixes the desktop-side reconnect after upgrade, and is unaffected by whether the old server's node child gets reaped cleanly.
- Probably also fires on any non-upgrade shutdown path that goes through `kill_running_server` (e.g. supervisor exit / quality switch), so the fix is generally useful.

(Written by Copilot)

title: Rubber Duck setting needs to be an application scoped setting (with current implementation)
body:
Testing https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/319092

`chat.agentHost.rubberDuck.enabled` is currently not implemented as a application scoped setting. The agent host will have the rubber duck feature enabled for all windows or none as there is only one agent host and not one per window so the setting can not be scoped by profile but has to be per application. If we think about remote agent host then even application scoped will not work.

I wonder why the rubber duck enablement needs to be set when the agent host starts and can not be sent with the request. It seems to me that this approach of specifying all settings when the agent host starts will cause a ton of issues long term. It will not allow for profile based settings and when it comes to remote agent host will complicate things even more. 

cc @roblourens @connor4312

title: Agents window with Remote SSH fails after session materialization: Copilot CLI session is not authenticated
body: Does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled?: No, requires Remote SSH, GitHub Copilot Chat, etc - VS Code Version: 1.122.0 - OS Version: Windows_NT x64 10.0.26200 (and the remote is Amazon Linux 2023) ## Summary When I use the Agents window with a Remote SSH host, the SSH connection and remote Agent Host appear to start successfully, and a provisional Copilot CLI session is created and materialized. However, the first message fails with a Copilot authentication error from the Agents Host output: ``` Session error: authentication - Execution failed: Error: Session was not created with authentication info or custom provider ``` Regular GitHub Copilot Chat works in VS Code, and Copilot CLI also works from the integrated terminal on the same remote host. The failure appears specific to the Agents window + Remote SSH + Copilot CLI Agent Host path. ## Steps to Reproduce: 1. Open the Agents window. 2. Select a Remote SSH host from the SSH config. 3. Select a folder on the remote host. 4. Start a new Copilot CLI session. 5. Send an arbitrary message, for example `Hello?`. ### Expected Result The message should be sent to the remote Copilot CLI session, and the Agents window should show the assistant response. ### Actual Result The session fails immediately after materialization. In VS Code 1.121.0, the authentication error was shown as a response message in the Agents window. In VS Code 1.122.0, the error is no longer shown in the session UI; instead, the session panel returns to the new-session panel immediately. The underlying error can be seen in the Agents Host output channel. The screenshot shows that the Agents window is connected to a Remote SSH workspace, Copilot CLI is selected, the remote file tree is visible, and the Agent Host terminal starts successfully on the remote host. ## Relevant Agents Host Output I redacted the remote host name and repository path. ``` [2026-05-28T05:01:02.195Z] [INFO ] [Copilot] Listing sessions... title: IAgentHostTerminalService and its implementation should live inside a terminalContrib
body:
Currently all this agent host stuff is mixed with the core terminal code. AFAICT it's only used in a select few places. The idea with `terminalContrib/` is to keep the core slim to reduce complexity. It enforces layer checks to ensure that it builds upon the core and not in it.

There are cases where things outside `terminalContrib/` need things inside which is why these re-exports exist:

https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/382e40aeea80678fe6bdb3ab9019f81c80f5d063/src/vs/workbench/contrib/terminal/terminalContribChatExports.ts#L1-L12

Ideally they wouldn't though, and to workaround that you could register the objects into something, rather than export the objects.

title: Improve Agent Host shell integration and command detection parity
body:
Context: 

Follow-up from #314189, specifically the closure note in https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/314189#issuecomment-4483218903.

The xterm/headless tracker served its purpose and the foundation pieces have landed, but several remaining terminal preview/output issues point at a narrower gap: Agent Host terminals have a lossy shell-integration bridge compared to local VS Code terminals.

Agent Host does inject VS Code shell integration into tool terminals, and it does parse core OSC 633 command lifecycle sequences. However, it currently parses/strips those sequences server-side and exposes only a reduced command lifecycle model to the client. The chat terminal preview then has to reconstruct command state from AHP terminal events, which is more fragile than local `CommandDetectionCapability`.

Related symptoms/issues:

- #313847: previous command output can be shown while the next command is running
- #317652: Run card reports "No output was produced by the command"
- #317106: terminal tool output not displayed
- #316976: streaming output rendering in the chat pane is inconsistent / duplicated / disappears
- #317558: Copilot agent "Failed to retrieve command output" with Oh My Posh + zsh on macOS
- #314926: prompt input can merge command output with the command
- #317423: Agent Host characters rendered incorrectly in htop

Well.. and this should be big part of the more generic issue: 
- #315191: broader rich terminal UI integration parity

**Problem**: 

Agent Host terminal command detection is currently a reduced reconstruction of local terminal shell integration. That loses some command-boundary context and creates room for incorrect command/output association.

<details>
<summary>How local workbench terminal shell integration works</summary>

The local workbench terminal receives the raw shell-integration stream in xterm and lets `ShellIntegrationAddon` / `CommandDetectionCapability` build rich command state from it.

At a high level:

1. PTY output flows directly into the visible xterm instance.
2. xterm's `ShellIntegrationAddon` consumes OSC 633 shell-integration sequences.
3. `CommandDetectionCapability` uses prompt start, command start, command line, command executed, command finished, cwd, rich-detection properties, and marker information to build local `ITerminalCommand` objects.
4. The raw shell-integration sequences are not rendered as normal text, but their semantic state remains available through terminal capabilities.
5. Local command execution can pre-bind a command id through `runCommand(..., commandId)` / `setNextCommandId`, making command-to-tool association more direct.

The important bit is that the local terminal consumes OSC 633 while preserving rich command detection state in the same terminal capability stack the rest of VS Code already uses.

</details>

<details>
<summary>How Agent Host terminal shell integration works today</summary>

Agent Host currently does something different:

1. The shell emits OSC 633 shell integration sequences.
2. `AgentHostTerminalManager` parses those sequences on the server.
3. The original OSC 633 sequences are stripped from `terminal/data` before the client receives terminal output.
4. AHP terminal state stores reduced command parts/events.
5. `AgentHostPty` replays synthetic marks for command boundaries.
6. `AhpTerminalCommandSource` reconstructs lightweight `ITerminalCommand` objects.
7. Chat terminal preview resolves command output using that reconstructed state.

This is intentional in one sense: Agent Host needs server-authoritative terminal state, and raw OSC 633 control bytes should not be treated as user-visible terminal output. The gap is that only part of the semantic shell-integration state is represented after stripping.

</details>

<details>
<summary>Current Agent Host OSC 633 coverage</summary>

Agent Host currently recognizes core OSC 633 events such as:

- `633;A` prompt start
- `633;B` command start
- `633;C` command executed / output begins
- `633;D` command finished
- `633;E` explicit command line
- `633;P;Cwd=...` cwd property

But the AHP client-facing model mostly exposes:

- command detection available
- command executed
- command finished
- cwd changed

Prompt start and command start are not preserved as rich command boundary state in the way local `CommandDetectionCapability` uses them.

Potentially missing or under-modeled shell-integration context includes:

- `633;P;HasRichCommandDetection=True`
- `633;F` / `633;G` continuation start/end
- `633;H` / `633;I` right prompt start/end
- `633;P;Prompt=...`, `PromptType=...`, `ContinuationPrompt=...`
- `633;P;IsWindows=True/False`
- `633;SetMark`
- environment reporting sequences, if we need parity there later

</details>

**Incremental steps to close this gap:**

1. Preserve OSC/data ordering
	- Split PTY chunks around shell-integration markers the way the local terminal path does before writing output.
	- Ensure output that arrives before `633;D` remains attached to the command that produced it.

2. Expose richer shell-integration metadata over AHP
	- Preserve prompt start, command start, command executed/output start, command finished, explicit command line, cwd, and command properties as semantic events/state.
	- Do not only collapse these into commandExecuted/commandFinished.

3. Make AHP command objects closer to local `ITerminalCommand`
	- Carry stable command ids, command line, cwd, exit code, marker/buffer positions, and output availability.
	- Let the AHP terminal command source resolve commands by authoritative AHP ids instead of reconstructing from reduced state.

4. Add an authoritative shell-tool to AHP-command association
	- Do not try to make local terminal command ids line up with Agent Host shell tools. Those are different PTYs/sources, so the hardening needs to happen inside the Agent Host path.
	- When a shell tool starts, keep the active `toolCallId`, `terminalUri`, command text, and any execution context in Agent Host server state.
	- When `AgentHostTerminalManager` observes `OSC 633;C` and creates the AHP terminal command id, record an explicit mapping from the shell tool call to `terminalUri + commandId`.
	- Expose that command id back through the shell tool/terminal metadata so chat progress can set `terminalCommandId` from the same AHP command stream that `AhpTerminalCommandSource` later reads.
	- Keep command-line matching only as a fallback, not as the primary association mechanism.

5. Avoid command-preview guessing
	- With an authoritative AHP command id, terminal preview should do an exact lookup against `AhpTerminalCommandSource.commands`.
	- If the id is missing, allow a strict fallback match against normalized tool input/command text for the same terminal.
	- If neither exists, prefer an unavailable/empty state instead of selecting the latest/previous command from the terminal.
	- This avoids showing output for the wrong shell-tool invocation while the deeper shell-integration parity work lands.

**IDEALLY:**

Agent Host shell tools should have command detection and shell-integration behavior close enough to local VS Code terminal tools that:

- terminal preview output is associated with the correct command
- completed command output, chat Run cards, and read-shell output agree
- prompt/command boundaries survive terminal replay and reconnect
- shell integration metadata is not lost merely because the OSC stream is consumed server-side

**Notes**

This is not saying Agent Host is missing `633;C` or `633;D`; those are parsed today. The issue is that the bridge is lossy: the original shell-integration stream is consumed on the server, but only a subset of the resulting command detection context is represented over AHP.


We should not try to make local terminal command ids magically line up with Agent Host shell tools. For Agent Host, we need an explicit server-side association between the shell tool call and the AHP terminal command id produced when OSC 633;C is observed. Shell integration parity makes those command ids/boundaries reliable; command-association hardening makes the preview use the right command.
