title: Agents: Add smoke test for Local Agent Host Provider harness
body:
[PR #317545](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/pull/317545) enabled smoke tests for the Copilot CLI, Claude, and Local harnesses from the Copilot Extension in the Agents Window. Each harness test opens the Agents Window against a mocked LLM server, creates a new session with the respective session type, sends "hello world", and validates the mocked response arrives end-to-end ΓÇö all without real GitHub auth or network calls.

With this in place, we can now detect sanity failures for each harness early ΓÇö if the session creation flow, model resolution, or chat request pipeline breaks for any session type, the smoke test will catch it before it reaches users.

**How it works:**
- Auth is bypassed using `VSCODE_COPILOT_CHAT_TOKEN` env var (fake token with `endpoints.api/proxy` pointing at the mock server) and `chat.allowAnonymousAccess` setting
- The Agents Window welcome/auth dialog is skipped via the existing `--enable-smoke-test-driver` flag
- The mock LLM server URL is injected into the Copilot SDK through `github.copilot.advanced.debug.overrideProxyUrl`, which redirects all SDK traffic (model fetching, chat completions, token refresh) to the mock
- For Claude, the mock server was extended with Anthropic Messages API SSE streaming support

**Ask:** Add a similar smoke test for the **Local Agent Host Provider** harness to complete the coverage across all session types.

Agents window: chat input silently dropped in "SSH in Workspace" mode; remote Copilot CLI never receives requests over the stdio tunnel
body:
<!-- ΓÜá∩╕ÅΓÜá∩╕Å Do Not Delete This! bug_report_template ΓÜá∩╕ÅΓÜá∩╕Å -->
<!-- Please read our Rules of Conduct: https://opensource.microsoft.com/codeofconduct/ -->
<!-- ≡ƒò« Read our guide about submitting issues: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/wiki/Submitting-Bugs-and-Suggestions -->
<!-- ≡ƒöÄ Search existing issues to avoid creating duplicates. -->
<!-- ≡ƒº¬ Test using the latest Insiders build to see if your issue has already been fixed: https://code.visualstudio.com/insiders/ -->
<!-- ≡ƒÆí Instead of creating your report here, use 'Report Issue' from the 'Help' menu in VS Code to pre-fill useful information. -->
<!-- ≡ƒöº Launch with `code --disable-extensions` to check. -->
Does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled?: Yes/No

<!-- ≡ƒ¬ô If you answered No above, use 'Help: Start Extension Bisect' from Command Palette to try to identify the cause. -->
<!-- ≡ƒôú Issues caused by an extension need to be reported directly to the extension publisher. The 'Help > Report Issue' dialog can assist with this. -->

Does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled?: Not applicable ΓÇö Agents window requires the GitHub Copilot Chat extension; disabling all extensions disables the feature under test.

  - VS Code Version: 1.120.0 (GitHub Copilot Chat 0.48.1)
  - OS Version: Windows 11 Enterprise 25H2 (build 26200.8246), WSL2 (Ubuntu, systemd enabled)

  ## Summary

  Selecting an SSH host as the workspace in the Agents window establishes the SSH connection, installs the Agent Host on the remote, and lets the user open an "Agent Host (<host>)" terminal that correctly drops into the remote shell ΓÇö but **typing in the chatbox does nothing**. No error surfaces in the
  UI, in the "GitHub Copilot Chat" Output channel, or in DevTools Console / Network. Remote Copilot CLI logs prove no request ever crosses the stdio tunnel.

  ## Steps to Reproduce

  1. From a Windows host with WSL2, set up sshd inside the WSL distro listening on `127.0.0.1:2222` with pubkey auth (this is the documented SSH-to-WSL workaround referenced in #312654). Confirm `ssh wsl` from PowerShell drops into the WSL shell with no prompts.
  2. In VS Code, open the Agents window (Command Palette ΓåÆ "Chat: Open Agents Window").
  3. In the workspace selector, choose Remote ΓåÆ SSH ΓåÆ `wsl` host. Pick a folder inside WSL (e.g. `/home/<user>/`).
  4. From the Agents window, open a new terminal and pick "Agent Host (wsl)". The terminal opens correctly inside WSL, confirming the SSH connection and Agent Host install succeeded.
  5. Type any text in the chatbox (e.g. `test`) and press Enter.

  ## Expected

  The chat request is dispatched to the remote Copilot CLI process over the SSH stdio tunnel, and a response appears in the Agents window.

  ## Actual

  Nothing happens. The input is accepted by the UI but no request is sent. No errors appear in the Agents window UI, in the "GitHub Copilot Chat" Output channel, or in DevTools Console / Network when Enter is pressed.

  ## Evidence the remote agent is healthy and idle

  `ps -ef` on the remote shows the full Agent Host chain running:

  - `~/.vscode-cli/code agent host --port 0`
  - `code-server ... --start-server --accept-server-license-terms --enable-remote-auto-shutdown`
  - `bootstrap-fork --type=agentHost --logsPath ~/.vscode-server/data/logs/...`
  - `node_modules/@github/copilot/index.js --headless --no-auto-update --log-level debug --stdio --auth-token-env COPILOT_SDK_AUTH_TOKEN --no-auto-login`

  Remote Copilot CLI log (`~/.copilot/logs/process-*.log`) shows it started cleanly and is waiting:

  ```
  [INFO] Starting CLI in server mode (stdio)
  [INFO] Starting CLI in stdio mode
  [DEBUG] Protocol methods registered
  [INFO] CLI server ready (stdio mode)
  [INFO] Server started, waiting for requests
  [DEBUG] [telemetry-queue] Flushing 1 events
  ```

  **No further lines appear after sending chat input ΓÇö i.e. no request ever reaches the remote CLI over stdio.**

  `~/.copilot/ide/` on the remote contains only a stale lock file from an earlier session; no new handshake artifact is written when the user sends a chat message.

  ## Evidence the chat session is bound to the local CLI, not the remote one

  The local "GitHub Copilot Chat" Output channel shows the MCP server starting against a **local Windows named pipe**, not a tunneled socket:

  ```
  [CopilotCLI] MCP server started. Lock file: C:\Users\<user>\.copilot\ide\<uuid>.lock
  [CopilotCLI] Server URI: pipe:%5C%5C.%5Cpipe%5Cmcp-<uuid>.sock#%2Fmcp
  ```

  This suggests the chat session is bound to the local Copilot CLI MCP socket while terminals are bound to the remote Agent Host ΓÇö so chat input never reaches the remote CLI that was selected as the workspace.

  ## Notes

  - Token SKU at repro: `free_limited_copilot`. Mentioning in case this is a gating issue rather than a routing bug ΓÇö though a user-visible error would be expected for a quota / SKU gate, not a silent no-op.
  - This is the SSH-to-WSL workaround documented for #312654 (Agents doesn't work well with WSL). The workaround appears to establish the connection and terminals correctly but does not wire the chat path to the remote agent.

title: Agent Host Incorrectly Constructs Git Worktree Path, Creating Directory in Wrong Location
body:
## Bug Description
When using "Open with Agents" feature, VS Code attempts to create a git worktree directory but constructs an invalid path. Instead of creating `~/.worktrees`, it tries to create `/home/username.worktrees` in the `/home/` directory where the user lacks write permissions.

## Error Message
```
Error: (sendFailed) Error: EACCES: permission denied, mkdir '/home/jarvismark1.worktrees'
```

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Start VS Code with Copilot CLI/Agent
2. 2. Use "Open with Agents" feature
3. 3. Request the agent to create a new project or workspace
## Expected Behavior
- Should create git worktree directory at `~/.worktrees` (i.e., `/home/jarvismark1/.worktrees`)
- - Path should be inside the user's home directory with proper write permissions
## Actual Behavior
- Attempts to create `/home/jarvismark1.worktrees` in `/home/` directory
- - Fails with `EACCES: permission denied` since user has no write permissions in `/home/`
- - - Agent becomes unusable
## Root Cause
In `agentHostMain.js`, the `K0()` function incorrectly constructs the path:

```javascript
function K0(i){
  return h.joinPath(i,"..",`${ge(i.fsPath)}.worktrees`)
}
```

This creates a path like `/home/` + `username.worktrees` instead of `/home/username/` + `.worktrees`

## Environment
- VS Code Version: Insiders (1.120.0+)
- - OS: Linux
- - - Platform: Linux x86_64
## Workaround
Manually create the directory with write permissions:
```bash
mkdir -p ~/.worktrees
chmod 755 ~/.worktrees
```

Agent SSH connection fails on Windows target due to Unix-only probe (uname -s)
body: Type: Bug Summary: When connecting an Agent session over SSH to a Windows host, connection fails because the Agent runs uname -s in a PowerShell shell. uname is not available in PowerShell, so the SSH check exits with code one. Reproduction Steps: Configure SSH host pointing to a Windows machine (OpenSSH server, default shell = PowerShell), for example windows-ssh-target. Start Agent remote connection over SSH. Observe connection failure during initial shell probe. Expected Behavior: Agent should detect Windows shells and use an OS-appropriate command (or shell-agnostic detection), allowing connection to succeed. Actual Behavior: Connection fails with: Failed to connect via SSH to windows-ssh-target. SSH command failed (exit one): uname -s. PowerShell error: The term 'uname' is not recognized. Error Output: Failed to connect via SSH to windows-ssh-target: Error: SSH command failed (exit one): uname -s stderr: uname: The term 'uname' is not recognized as a name of a cmdlet, function, script file, or executable program. Check the spelling of the name, or if a path was included, verify that the path is correct, or try again. Environment: Local OS: Windows Remote host OS: Windows Remote shell: PowerShell (OpenSSH default shell) VS Code Remote SSH usage: Works in normal VS Code workflow Agent over SSH: Fails during Unix shell probe Impact: Blocks Agent usage for Windows SSH targets unless the remote default shell is changed to Bash. This is a compatibility gap for valid Windows OpenSSH plus PowerShell setups. Workarounds Tried: VS Code SSH connection works separately. Agent flow still fails at Unix probe command. Switching remote OpenSSH default shell to Bash appears to be a possible workaround, but should not be required for Windows support. Suggested Fix: Replace hardcoded uname -s probe with shell or OS-neutral detection. Add explicit Windows PowerShell fallback detection path. Treat probe failure as recoverable and try alternate detection commands. VS Code version: Code 1.120.0 (0958016b2af9f09bb4257e0df4a95e2f90590f9f, 2026-05-12T20:17:22Z) OS version: Windows_NT x64 10.0.26100 Modes: Remote OS version: Windows_NT x64 10.0.19044

title: Clarify pty-host ownership boundaries for Agent Host custom PTYs
body:
Follow-up from https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/pull/316482.

That PR fixes one concrete case where Agent Host/custom PTY title and icon changes were propagated to the pty host even though Agent Host terminals are owned by `AgentHostTerminalManager`, not by the normal terminal pty host / `PtyService`.

The confusing part is that `persistentProcessId` can exist for a custom PTY, but that does not necessarily mean the id is present in `PtyService._ptys`:

```text
Regular workbench terminal:
  TerminalService / TerminalProcessManager
    -> pty host backend
    -> PtyService._ptys[persistentProcessId]

Agent Host terminal:
  TerminalService / TerminalProcessManager
    -> AgentHostPty customPtyImplementation
    -> Agent Host protocol
    -> AgentHostTerminalManager._terminals[terminalUri]
```

Follow-up ideas:

- Audit remaining `persistentProcessId` uses that call into the pty host backend and confirm they exclude `customPtyImplementation` terminals where needed.
- Consider adding a clearer helper/name for ΓÇ£pty-host-backed terminalΓÇ¥ so future code does not treat any `persistentProcessId` as a `PtyService` id.
- Decide whether Agent Host needs a protocol/UI path for dynamic icon changes, similar to the existing `terminal/titleChanged` path, instead of using `PtyService.updateIcon`.

title: Agent skill file path uses client OS path instead of remote host path over Remote SSH
body:
## Problem

When using Copilot Chat with agent skills over Remote SSH (Windows client -> Linux remote), the agent receives the skill file path resolved using the **client OS convention** (Windows path), not the remote host filesystem path. The file read fails silently, and the agent falls back to degraded behavior without the skill context.

## Reproduction

1. Install a Copilot agent plugin that defines a skill (e.g., via `external.json` in an extension pack -- no OS-specific paths in the definition)
2. Connect to a Linux remote host via Remote SSH from a Windows client
3. The skill/plugin is installed on the remote at `~/.copilot/installed-plugins/_direct/<publisher>--<name>/skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md`
4. Invoke the skill in Copilot Chat

## Expected

The agent receives the skill file path relative to the **remote host filesystem**, e.g.:
```
/home/<user>/.copilot/installed-plugins/_direct/microsoft--Build-CLI/skills/microsoft-build/SKILL.md
```

## Actual

The agent receives a **Windows client path**:
```
c:/Users/<user>/.vscode/agent-plugins/github.com/microsoft/Build-CLI/skills/microsoft-build/SKILL.md
```

This path does not exist on the Linux remote. The file read fails. The agent does not recover (no fallback search for the file on disk), and proceeds without the skill context.

## Impact

The skill loaded by name and appeared in the available skills list, but its instructions were never read. In this case it was a Build session catalog skill -- the agent fell back to docs-only search and missed the entire session catalog (the primary output of the skill). The same file was read successfully by Copilot CLI running directly on the same Linux host.

## Environment

- VS Code with Remote SSH extension
- Client: Windows
- Remote: Linux
- Copilot Chat with agent mode
- Plugin defined via `external.json` (no OS-specific paths in the plugin definition)

title: Allow Claude Agent to run through Anthropic subscription instead of Copilot quota
body: I would like VS Code to support using **Claude Agent (Preview)** directly through an **Anthropic / Claude subscription or Anthropic API credentials**, instead of only consuming the userΓÇÖs **GitHub Copilot subscription**. This feature request comes from a real misunderstanding I had while using the feature. Since Claude Agent is described as using the official Anthropic agent harness / Claude Agent SDK, I initially assumed that the Claude usage would be tied to my existing Claude/Anthropic subscription, or at least that there would be a way to use it directly through Anthropic. The confusion mainly came from the agent/model selector, where seeing Claude Agent listed made me assume that it could be used through my Claude/Anthropic subscription. Because I misunderstood this billing model, I ended up spending around **$300 extra in requests in a single week**. This is not meant as a complaint, but as context for why making the billing/authentication model more explicit, or supporting direct Anthropic usage, would be valuable. Ideally, Claude Agent could offer a provider/authentication option such as: - Use via GitHub Copilot subscription - Use via Anthropic / Claude subscription - Use via Anthropic API key or Claude Code authentication This would allow users to choose explicitly how Claude Agent usage is authenticated and billed. If supporting direct Anthropic authentication inside the existing Claude Agent option is not desirable, I would also see it as a viable alternative to add a separate option to the current agent/model selector, clearly indicating that this version runs through the userΓÇÖs Anthropic/Claude subscription instead of Copilot. For example, the selector could include two clearly separated entries: - Claude Agent ΓÇö via Copilot subscription - Claude Agent ΓÇö via Anthropic subscription This would make the billing model explicit and avoid confusion, while still preserving the current Copilot-based integration as the default or recommended option. From a user perspective, this seems potentially feasible, since the existing Claude extension for VS Code already allows users to work with Claude through their own Claude/Anthropic access. I understand there may be product, technical, or legal considerations on the VS Code/Copilot side, but having a similar option in the integrated chat/agent selector would be much more convenient. The main benefit would be that developers who already use Claude or Claude Code could use the full Claude Agent SDK experience inside VS Code without consuming Copilot quota or premium requests. It would also make the integration more flexible and clearer, especially for users who want to keep Copilot and Anthropic usage separate. Even if direct Anthropic authentication is not planned, it would be very helpful to make the billing/usage model more explicit in the UI, so users do not mistakenly assume that Claude Agent usage is covered by their Claude subscription.

title: AgentHost: Use AHP completions as source of truth for Agent Host chat input
body:
(Codex) 

## Problem

Agent Host sessions currently still rely on VS Code chat input completion/contribution plumbing in places. This can diverge between the new chat widget and the standard chat widget, and it also means VS Code may offer completions or attachment affordances that the connected Agent Host session does not actually support.

For Agent Host sessions, the Agent Host should be the source of truth for supported completions and attachable references.

## Context

In the attachment/completion protocol work, AHP added a completions request that can return attachment-shaped completion items. For Agent Host sessions, we should use AHP completions as the source of truth instead of relying on most built-in VS Code chat contributions.

This is broader than the specific selection attachment bug tracked in:

- https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/314184

It is also related to image/blob attachment support:

- https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/314243

Protocol support landed 
## Expected Behavior

For Agent Host-backed chat sessions:

- Slash command completions should come from the Agent Host/AHP session.
- File/symbol/attachment completions should come from the Agent Host/AHP session where applicable.
- VS Code should avoid showing built-in chat completions that are not supported by the current Agent Host.
- The new chat widget and standard chat widget should behave consistently for Agent Host sessions.
- The completion result should preserve enough information to create the correct AHP attachment/request payload.

## Likely Work

- Sync the latest AHP protocol types into VS Code.
- Ensure Agent Host sessions are available early enough for chat input completion requests.
- Route Agent Host chat input completion requests to the AHP completions command.
- Filter or bypass built-in VS Code chat completions for Agent Host sessions where AHP should own the result.
- Convert returned AHP completion items into the chat input UI's completion/attachment model.
- Add tests for slash command completions and attachment completions in Agent Host sessions.

## Related

- https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/314184
- https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/314243

## Classification

Agent Host implementation gap. AHP now has protocol support for richer attachments and completions; VS Code still needs to make Agent Host/AHP the source of truth for completion behavior in Agent Host sessions.

/cc @connor4312

title: AgentHost: Support image attachments for Copilot CLI sessions
body:
## Problem

The existing Copilot CLI chat-panel path supports image attachments, but the Agent Host path does not appear to build or forward image/blob attachments when sending Copilot CLI requests through Agent Host.

Now that Agent Host Protocol has richer attachment support, VS Code should wire image attachments through the Agent Host path so the public Copilot SDK route can preserve parity with the current Copilot CLI-in-chat experience.

## Current Context

- Agent Host Protocol now supports richer attachments via https://github.com/microsoft/agent-host-protocol/pull/95.
- The protocol PR explicitly mentions embedded resources and image support.
- The migration plan tracks this as an AHP implementation gap: public Copilot SDK can accept blob attachments, but AHP/VS Code currently does not build them.
- Related selection attachment issue: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/314184.

## Expected Behavior

When a user sends a Copilot CLI request through Agent Host with image attachments, the Agent Host path should preserve and forward the image data to the Copilot SDK route.

This should support the same user-facing image attachment scenarios that work in the existing Copilot CLI chat-panel integration.

## Likely Fix

After syncing the latest Agent Host Protocol attachment types into VS Code:

- Convert chat image attachments/variables into the richer Agent Host attachment shape.
- Preserve image data and MIME type through the Agent Host request path.
- Map the Agent Host image/blob attachment into the public Copilot SDK blob attachment shape.
- Add focused tests covering image attachment conversion and SDK payload shape.

## Related

- https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/314184
- https://github.com/microsoft/agent-host-protocol/pull/95

## Classification

Agent Host implementation gap. Protocol support has landed, and public Copilot SDK can represent blob/image attachments; the remaining work is VS Code Agent Host wiring.

title: Explore xterm/headless for Agent Host terminal state tracking
body:
## Context

Agent Host currently operates close to the PTY stream for terminal tool handling. That gives us raw terminal data, but it does not necessarily give us the same interpreted terminal state that a user sees in VS Code. Some of the terminal polish issues we have been discussing, such as multiline command handling, escaping, idle detection, and resize behavior, may be easier to reason about if Agent Host also maintains an xterm-style terminal buffer.


Potential benefits: 

- Better multiline command handling: preserve the terminal's interpreted view of wrapped lines, continuation prompts, pasted newlines, and shell-editing state instead of reasoning only from raw PTY bytes.
- Bracketed paste mode support: detect when the shell/application has enabled bracketed paste and send pasted or multiline input in a way shells understand, reducing surprising execution or escaping behavior.
- More accurate prompt and idleness detection: use terminal-buffer state to distinguish "command is still running" from "shell is ready", including prompts that are visually present but hard to infer from raw output.
- Escape sequence interpretation: let xterm handle ANSI/VT sequences, cursor movement, line clearing, carriage returns, progress spinners, color/control codes, and similar terminal behavior.
- Better handling of interactive terminal UIs: even if full TUI steering is out of scope, a headless buffer could provide more faithful snapshots for full-screen apps, alternate screen usage, menus, pagers, and editors.
- Terminal resize behavior: maintain a terminal model with rows/cols, wrapping, and reflow semantics, which may help debug or fix resize-loop and layout issues.
- Shared heuristics with the workbench terminal: make it more plausible to reuse or faithfully port logic that assumes xterm-like buffer access, such as prompt-pattern detection and idle heuristics.
- Cleaner separation of raw PTY data from interpreted terminal state: Agent Host could keep both the raw stream and a "what the user would see" view for tool display and lifecycle decisions.
- Better transcript quality: captured command output could avoid duplicate/control-sequence noise and more closely reflect the visible terminal.
- Foundation for future terminal features: create a place to build richer terminal-state events, snapshots, diagnostics, and safer command injection behavior over time.

Other big thing is responding to terminal query such as DSR, otherwise user would end up seeing weird escape sequences in their agent host terminal. 

Relevant issues atm and current pain points

- [AgentHost: Multiline shell command not working in tool](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/312922): a headless xterm buffer could help preserve the interpreted terminal state around pasted newlines, wrapped commands, continuation prompts, and shell editing behavior.
- [AgentHost: Issue with ! in shell command](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/312009): the Slack discussion also called out escaping issues and the fact that the Agent Host terminal tool does not have the same idleness detection smarts as the built-in terminal tool. A terminal model that understands the visible buffer and shell state may make this easier to reason about.
- [Agent host terminal rendering issue](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/312876): a headless xterm layer would maintain rows, columns, wrapping, reflow, cursor movement, and terminal rendering state, which may help diagnose or address resize/rendering loops.



Copilot CLI already does this for their shell tools, we should also, since we'd be overriding their shell tool in agent host scenario.

title: AgentHost: Selection attachments drop selected text and range
body:
(Codex) 

## Problem

The Copilot CLI chat-panel path preserves editor selection context when sending a request: it forwards the selected file, selected text, and selection range.

The Agent Host path currently converts an implicit selection into a selection attachment, but only includes the file URI and display name. The selected text and range are dropped before the request reaches the Copilot SDK.

## Current Context

There are incoming agent host protocol changes for richer attachments. This issue should track the VS Code-side implementation work after those changes land, rather than extending the current older `file` / `directory` / `selection` attachment shape directly.

## Evidence

- `src/vs/workbench/contrib/chat/browser/agentSessions/agentHost/agentHostSessionHandler.ts`
  - `_convertVariablesToAttachments` handles implicit selections, but emits only `{ type, uri, displayName }` today.
- `src/vs/platform/agentHost/common/agentService.ts`
  - `IAgentAttachment` already has optional `text` and `selection` fields in the current VS Code-side interface.
- `src/vs/platform/agentHost/node/copilot/copilotAgentSession.ts`
  - `CopilotAgentSession.send` already forwards `text` and `selection` to the public Copilot SDK if present.
- `extensions/copilot/src/extension/chatSessions/copilotcli/node/copilotcliPromptResolver.ts`
  - The existing chat-panel CLI path preserves richer selection context.

## Expected Behavior

When a user sends a Copilot CLI request through Agent Host with editor selection context, the Agent Host path should preserve:

- selected file URI/path
- selected text
- selection start/end line and character

This should match the current Copilot CLI chat-panel behavior and avoid losing important context after moving CLI integration to Agent Host.

## Likely Fix

After the richer attachment shape lands, wire editor selection attachments through the VS Code Agent Host path:

- Convert implicit editor selection variables into the richer selection attachment shape.
- Preserve selected text and selection range when converting chat variables in `AgentHostSessionHandler`.
- Preserve those fields through Agent Host side effects/state.
- Map the richer Agent Host selection attachment into the Copilot SDK selection attachment shape in `CopilotAgentSession.send`.

## Classification

Agent Host implementation gap. The public Copilot SDK already accepts selection `text` and `selection` fields; the remaining work is VS Code-side Agent Host wiring after the incoming agent host protocol changes land.

title: Replace `AgentHostConfigKey.DefaultShell` magic key with a typed clientΓåÆhost channel
body:
Follow-up to #313160 / #313679. Raised in PR review by @connor4312.

Today the workbench tells the agent host which shell to spawn by writing the magic key `AgentHostConfigKey.DefaultShell = 'defaultShell'` into the agent host's root-config bag via `RootConfigChanged`:

```ts
// In agentHostTerminalContribution.ts
this._agentHostService.dispatch({
    type: ActionType.RootConfigChanged,
    config: { [AgentHostConfigKey.DefaultShell]: profile.path },
});
```

ΓÇªand the host reads the same string-literal key:

```ts
// In agentHostTerminalManager.ts
const configured = this._configurationService.getRootValue(
    agentHostCustomizationConfigSchema,
    AgentHostConfigKey.DefaultShell
);
```

This conflates two unrelated concerns into a single config bag:

- **User-editable host customizations** (`customizations: [...]`) ΓÇö meant for the user to tweak through the AICustomizationManagementEditor; the host owns the schema, clients suggest values.
- **Workbench-internal clientΓåÆhost plumbing** (`defaultShell`, future client-controlled values) ΓÇö should not be visible to the user as editable config; not domain-meaningful for end users.

The two sides are wired together by an implicit string-name handshake (`'defaultShell'`) rather than a typed RPC contract. Renaming on one side silently breaks the other. There's no compile-time link between `_pushDefaultShell` (the writer) and `getDefaultShell` (the reader).

### Proposed direction

Replace the magic-key dispatch with a dedicated typed channel. Three options, in increasing order of impact:

#### (a) Typed RPC method on `IAgentConnection`

```ts
interface IAgentConnection {
    setShellConfig(config: { path: string }): Promise<void>;
}
```

Workbench calls a typed method; host has a typed handler. Drop `AgentHostConfigKey.DefaultShell` from the customization schema (or keep deprecated for one release for back-compat).

Pros: smallest wire change; keeps customization bag clean for actual customizations.
Cons: still a one-off RPC; doesn't generalize if more clientΓåÆhost signals arrive.

#### (b) Dedicated AHP action type

```ts
type ClientShellConfigChangedAction = {
    type: 'client/shellConfigChanged';
    shell: { path: string };
};
```

Cleanly separated from `root/configChanged`. Schema-validated. Multiple client-pushed signals naturally extend by adding more action types in the same family.

Pros: protocol-level separation; consistent with existing AHP action patterns.
Cons: more AHP-protocol churn; needs Swift bindings updated.

#### (c) Sub-bag separation

Split the root-config bag into `root/customizations` (user-editable) and `root/clientConfig` (workbench-pushed). Existing `RootConfigChanged` continues to write the customizations bag; new `RootClientConfigChanged` writes the plumbing bag.

Pros: minimal protocol surface change; backward compat possible.
Cons: still bag-shaped; doesn't fully address the typed-RPC concern.

### Cross-repo implications

This touches the AHP protocol repo (`microsoft/agent-host-protocol`):
- New action / method definition.
- Schema-generator updates.
- Regenerated Swift bindings (Swift client recently shipped support for `rootConfigChanged` ΓÇö see microsoft/agent-host-protocol#91).
- Headless `code agent-host` daemon needs to honor the new channel.

Coordinate with @rebornix on the Swift side and @connor4312 on AHP design before implementation.

### Migration plan sketch

1. Introduce the new typed channel in the AHP repo.
2. Update host (`getDefaultShell`) to read from the new channel, falling back to the customization-bag key during the migration window.
3. Update workbench dispatch site to write to the new channel.
4. After one release: drop the customization-bag fallback and remove `AgentHostConfigKey.DefaultShell` from the schema.

### Recommendation

Start with **(a)**. It's the smallest change that eliminates the magic-key handshake and frees the customization bag from non-customization keys. (b) and (c) become natural follow-ons if more clientΓåÆhost signals arrive.

/cc @connor4312 @rebornix #313160 #313679

title: Support shell-profile config for remote agent hosts (`IRemoteAgentHostService` connections)
body:
Follow-up to #313160 / #313679.

#313679 made the local agent host respect `terminal.integrated.agentHostProfile.<os>` (with fallback to `defaultProfile.<os>`). Remote agent hosts (`IRemoteAgentHostService` connections ΓÇö SSH / tunnel / WebSocket-relayed) currently fall through to `getSystemShell()` on their own machine. There's no user-controllable way to pick the shell for a remote agent host today.

The push side intentionally does not fan out to remote connections because the workbench-resolved path is local-machine-shaped (e.g. `C:\Program Files\Git\bin\bash.exe`) and not necessarily valid on the remote machine.

Three approaches, in increasing order of complexity. My vote is **(a)**.

### (a) Server-side config on the remote ΓÇö recommended

The remote `code agent-host` already reads `defaultShell` directly from `agent-host-config.json` (see `agentHostMain.ts` ΓåÆ `rootConfigResource = appSettingsHome/globalStorage/agent-host-config.json`). The mechanism exists; the gap is documentation and discoverability.

Work:
- [ ] Document the file location and key in our docs / `AGENTS.md` / a `code agent-host --help` mention.
- [ ] Optionally add a `code agent-host --default-shell <path>` CLI flag that writes the value (or a one-shot `code agent-host set-shell <path>`).
- [ ] Confirm the path is `~/.vscode-cli/agent-host-state/agent-host-config.json` (or whatever the canonical location is) for SSH / tunnel deployments.

Pros: zero workbench changes; path is set on the machine that owns the PTY (always valid); consistent with how server admins already configure.
Cons: not in Settings UI; per-machine setup.

### (b) Per-address mapping setting in the workbench

```jsonc
"remote.agentHost.shellByAddress": {
  "ssh-server-1.foo.com": "/usr/bin/fish",
  "tunnel-relay.bar.net":   "/usr/local/bin/zsh"
}
```

Workbench iterates `IRemoteAgentHostService.connections`, looks up each connection's address, and dispatches the matching value to that connection.

Pros: surfaced in Settings UI; user-discoverable; workbench-driven.
Cons: user has to know the remote-side path; settings UX for object maps is clunky; per-connection schema-gate-and-onDidChange wiring.

### (c) Remote-side resolver

Reproduce `ITerminalProfileResolverService` on the agent host side: read remote-side settings.json, resolve profile by name, etc. Same UX as local case but for remote.

Pros: fully consistent with how vscode-remote handles this; future-proof for args/env.
Cons: large new surface area; new AHP wire shape; more tests.

### Out of scope for this issue

- Args/env support ΓÇö tracked separately in #313790.
- Replacing the `defaultShell` magic-key dispatch with a typed RPC channel ΓÇö separate follow-up.

/cc #313160 #313679

title: Pipe terminal-profile `args` (and decide on `env`) to the agent host
body:
Follow-up to #313160 / #313679.

#313679 made the agent host respect the user's preferred shell **executable** by piping `terminal.integrated.agentHostProfile.<os>` (with fallback to `defaultProfile.<os>`) down to the agent host as `AgentHostConfigKey.DefaultShell`. Only the `path` is honored today ΓÇö `args` and `env` from the profile are silently dropped.

That means a user with, say:

```jsonc
"terminal.integrated.profiles.windows": {
    "Git Bash": { "source": "Git Bash", "args": ["--login", "-i"] }
}
```

ΓÇªgets `bash.exe` spawned in the agent host without `--login -i`, so their `~/.bash_profile` doesn't run, PATH tweaks/aliases/exports the user expects don't apply. Same story for `--noprofile`, custom `--rcfile`, oh-my-zsh-style `-i`, etc. macOS bash/zsh `--login` is the only argument added automatically today (in `AgentHostTerminalManager.createTerminal`).

Reasons we deferred this on #313679:

1. **Args splicing has order-sensitivity with shell-integration injection.** `getShellIntegrationInjection` overwrites `shellArgs` with its own `newArgs` (e.g. `--init-file ΓÇª` for bash, `-NoProfile -Command "& ΓÇª"` for pwsh). Mixing user args needs per-shell-family rules (before the integration script for bash, before `-Command` for pwsh, etc.).
2. **Env merging needs an explicit precedence policy** with the agent host's curated env. We already set `LC_ALL=C.UTF-8`, `PAGER=''`, `GIT_TERMINAL_PROMPT=0`, `DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive`, `VSCODE_PREVENT_SHELL_HISTORY`, plus shell integration's `envMixin`. User-profile env (e.g. `LC_ALL=en_US.UTF-8`, `PAGER=less`, custom `PROMPT_COMMAND`) can break agent output parsing if merged naively.
3. **The single-string `defaultShell` wire shape** stays clean for headless `code agent-host` and SSH/tunnel agent hosts, where workbench profile semantics don't apply.

What this issue should cover:

- [ ] Extend the AHP root-config schema to carry args (`AgentHostConfigKey.DefaultShellArgs` as a sibling string-array key, keeping `DefaultShell` as-is for back-compat).
- [ ] Workbench bridge in `AgentHostTerminalContribution._pushDefaultShell` resolves and pushes both keys.
- [ ] `AgentHostTerminalManager.createTerminal` reads the configured args from root config and splices them in correctly relative to shell-integration injection.
- [ ] Decide on env policy. Options: (a) ignore profile env entirely (current behavior), (b) whitelist a small set, (c) merge with agent-host curated env winning. Probably (a) or (b).
- [ ] Update setting descriptions and `AgentHostConfigKey.DefaultShell` JSDoc to remove the "args/env are ignored" caveat (or update it to reflect the new behavior).
- [ ] Tests for args splicing per shell family.

/cc #313160 #313679

title: Agent host: support GitHub Enterprise (GHE) for Copilot auth
body:
Today the agent host's Copilot auth path is hardcoded to github.com end-to-end. To use Copilot from a GHE instance, two layers need to change in lockstep:

## 1. Agent host: protected-resource metadata is github.com-only

The Copilot agent's `getProtectedResources()` only advertises github.com:

```ts
getProtectedResources(): ProtectedResourceMetadata[] {
    return [{
        resource: 'https://api.github.com',
        resource_name: 'GitHub Copilot',
        authorization_servers: ['https://github.com/login/oauth'],
        scopes_supported: ['read:user', 'user:email'],
        required: true,
    }];
}
```

This drives which OAuth server the CLI / client performs the device-code flow against. For a GHE user it needs to advertise `https://<ghe-host>/api/v3` (or equivalent) and `https://<ghe-host>/login/oauth`. The GHE host should be **configurable by the agent host operator** (config file / env / launch flag) so a single agent-host binary can be pointed at any GHE instance without a rebuild.

## 2. `CopilotApiService`: token mint URL is hardcoded to `defaultChatAgent.tokenEntitlementUrl`

See the TODO in `src/vs/platform/agentHost/node/shared/copilotApiService.ts` (`_buildCapiInit`):

```ts
// TODO(GHE): For GitHub Enterprise users the mint URL must point to
// `api.<enterprise-host>/copilot_internal/v2/token` instead.
const tokenUrl = this._productService.defaultChatAgent.tokenEntitlementUrl;
```

For GHE this needs to point at `api.<ghe-host>/copilot_internal/v2/token` instead of `api.github.com/...`. The enterprise host has to thread through the public `ICopilotApiService` surface (extra param on `messages`/`models`, a per-host instance, or a `create(enterpriseHost?)` factory) ΓÇö `defaultAccount.ts` in the workbench already derives this URL the same way for the main auth path and is a good reference.

## Why this is one issue

(1) and (2) are coupled ΓÇö fixing only the mint URL doesn't help if the OAuth flow still hits github.com, and vice versa. Both should land together with a shared "GHE host" configuration source.

## Out of scope

- The Copilot CLI's own OAuth handling (Rust, in `cli/`) is github.com-hardcoded but is a separate consumer; this issue is specifically about the agent host being usable against a GHE Copilot.

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

title: Duplicate chat session after host disconnects
body:
1. Go to insiders.vscode.dev/agents 2. Create a new session and send something 3. Once it's done force a reconnection on the host 4. Once the host is back online the client reconnects 5. Without changing sessions try sending a new request to the same opened chat 6. ≡ƒÉ¢ A new session item is created instead of using the same chat ``` mermaid sequenceDiagram autonumber actor User participant CS as ChatService participant Ctrl as ListController participant H1 as Handler (pre-reconnect) participant H2 as Handler (post-reconnect) participant BE as Agent Host rect rgb(255, 235, 235) User->>CS: New chat ΓåÆ URI /untitled-abc User->>CS: send "first" CS->>Ctrl: createNewChatSessionItem Note over Ctrl: no newChatSessionItem
returns undefined CS->>H1: _invokeAgent(/untitled-abc) H1->>BE: createSession() BE-->>H1: backend /uuid-1 H1->>H1: _sessionToBackend.set(
/untitled-abc ΓåÆ /uuid-1) H1-->>User: "first" reply Note over H1: ≡ƒÆÑ tunnel reconnect
handler disposed,
map lost H1-xH1: dispose User->>CS: send "second" (still /untitled-abc) CS->>H2: _invokeAgent(/untitled-abc) Note over H2: cache miss
URI is untitled H2->>BE: createSession() AGAIN BE-->>H2: ≡ƒÉ¢ NEW backend /uuid-2 H2-->>User: "second" lands on a fresh session
history orphaned end ``` There's a lot of things involved here that I'm not sure the intention behind: 1. [The agentHostSessionHandler](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/44dda2c61e5af96b0926a5a3ca7c9973005e507e/src/vs/workbench/contrib/chat/browser/agentSessions/agentHost/agentHostSessionHandler.ts#L347-L348) keeps an in-memory map of the frontend session to a backend session. Can we just always rely on the sessions the backend nows about? If the AgentHostSessionHandler is disposed then the client doesn't know anymore what sessions is talking to in the backend 2. [We are creating an untitled session](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/44dda2c61e5af96b0926a5a3ca7c9973005e507e/src/vs/sessions/contrib/agentHost/browser/baseAgentHostSessionsProvider.ts#L522-L523) and setting it as the active session, even if the agent host already created a real session behind the scenes, the client still holds the reference of the old resource. If the host already knows how to create a session why do we need to create an untitled one? 3. This behavior seems to only impact when creating a session from the welcome screen, looks like we do the right thing when creating/delegating a session from chat and the reason I think it's because we do it [this other way](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/fbce3ede577c6378adf6b5cd3107c230826622a1/src/vs/workbench/contrib/chat/browser/agentSessions/agentHost/agentHostSessionHandler.ts#L2166-L2173)

title: agents: ssh ProxyCommand
body:
SSH is not working with some SSH config files.

1. Create a GitHub Codespace.
2. Using the GH CLI, add the code space to your local `~/.ssh/config`:

   ```bash
   gh codespace ssh --config > ~/.ssh/config
   ```

   which will append something like:

   ```
   Host cs.smiles-kittens-sunshine-12873.HEAD
	User codespace
	ProxyCommand /opt/homebrew/bin/gh cs ssh -c smiles-kittens-sunshine-12873 --stdio -- -i /Users/steve/.ssh/codespaces.auto
	UserKnownHostsFile=/dev/null
	StrictHostKeyChecking no
	LogLevel quiet
	ControlMaster auto
	IdentityFile /Users/steve/.ssh/codespaces.auto
   ```

3. In a local terminal, confirm you can actually ssh:

   ```bash
   ssh cs.smiles-kittens-sunshine-12873.HEAD
   ```

   observe this drops you into a shell and you can successfully SSH.

4. Now, in the Agents app, go through the SSH flow. You can see the host (`cs.smiles-kittens-sunshine-12873.HEAD`) pre-populated, but when Agents attempts to connect to it, it's failing to resolve an address likely caused by not doing the ProxyCommand.

title: Respect the user's preferred shell in agent host terminals
body: Local, remotes are more complicated than I thought, created separate follow up issues, references below in history. Today, host-managed terminals in the agent host (the PTY-backed `bash`/`powershell` tools in [`copilotShellTools.ts`](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/main/src/vs/platform/agentHost/node/copilot/copilotShellTools.ts)) hardcode the shell ΓÇö on Windows that's always windows pwsh (pwsh 5) regardless of what the user has set up. > If I have PowerShell 7 installed I think agent host terminal would still go to windows powershell (pwsh5) Making the default better (well... some windows users are passionate git bash users all time) and also respecting user's preferred shell (such as the one defined in setting) would be great. We allow user to set per OS like: ` "terminal.integrated.defaultProfile.windows":` - **(a)** Inherit the existing integrated-terminal settings ΓÇö `terminal.integrated.defaultProfile.*` - The agent host can't read workbench config directly?? because it's a separate Node process, but the workbench-side `NodeAgentHostStarter` already has `IConfigurationService` and forwards data to the child via env vars and the AHP root-config bag ΓÇö same channel `customizations` uses today. So the workbench resolves the profile via `ITerminalProfileResolverService` and pushes the resolved path/args down on startup and on setting change. - **(b)** Add a dedicated `agentHost.defaultShell` setting. Simple and isolated, but creates a parallel surface users have to configure separately. - **(c)** Both ΓÇö dedicated setting wins if explicitly set, otherwise inherit from `terminal.integrated.*`. Maybe we'd have dedicated setting for agent host terminal, but if the value is not set there we'll respect user's default terminal profile setting. Existing behavior on Copilot CLI on chat panel:

title: SSH - Visual Studio Code Agents - Does not support keys with passphrases on Windows
body:
Type: Bug The new SSH2 based SSH workspace connection within Visual Studio Code Agents does not work with keys that contain a passphrase on Windows 11. SSH connections continue work fine with the SSH remote extension in Visual Studio Code and Visual Studio Code Insiders on Windows. The specific use-case is Windows hosting VSCode; A local VM running Ubuntu 24.04. To clarify, only the Visual Studio Code Agents GUI exhibits this failure and this failure prevents the new GUI from being utilised with this setup as it cannot connect to the remote. Reproduction steps: - Load Visual Studio Code Agents - "Start by Picking a " -> "Manage..." -> "SSH" -> "name of the remote" (which appears to have been read from the existing SSH config in the Windows users .ssh directory). - Result: Debug logging appears to be limited via this alternate SSH2 flow as well. The debug level in the .ssh/config file is not honoured. There doesn't appear to be a setting to increase the SSH logging verbosity. The code in sshRemoteAgentHostService.ts doesn't appear to support the Windows named pipe agent and depends on SSH_AUTH_SOCK which is not defined by the ssh agent on Windows (which uses a named pipe instead). When an encrypted key is utilised, there doesn't appear to be a codepath to prompt for the key passphrase either. This is a regression compared to the behaviour of the remote SSH extension. Logging: To clarify: - ssh remote-name -> works fine from the Windows terminal. - Utilising the SSH remote extension in VSCode and VSCode Insiders continues to work fine. title: Agent host session subscriptions are never released after the chat is closed
body:
## Problem

Once the user opens an agent host session in the chat panel, the `IAgentSubscription<ISessionState>` acquired by [`AgentHostSessionHandler`](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/main/src/vs/workbench/contrib/chat/browser/agentSessions/agentHost/agentHostSessionHandler.ts) is never released — it stays open for the lifetime of the window (or until the chat content provider unregisters).

Trace:

1. `provideChatSessionContent` calls `_ensureSessionSubscription(resolvedSession)` (around [agentHostSessionHandler.ts#L450](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/main/src/vs/workbench/contrib/chat/browser/agentSessions/agentHost/agentHostSessionHandler.ts#L450)) and stores the released ref in the `AgentHostChatSession`'s onDispose callback ([line ~526](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/main/src/vs/workbench/contrib/chat/browser/agentSessions/agentHost/agentHostSessionHandler.ts#L526)).
2. The `IChatSession` is cached by `ChatSessionsService._sessions` in [`getOrCreateChatSession`](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/main/src/vs/workbench/contrib/chat/browser/chatSessions/chatSessions.contribution.ts#L1012).
3. The cache entry is only disposed when (a) the content provider unregisters, (b) a contribution's when-clause turns it off, or (c) the session itself fires `onWillDispose`. **There is no editor-close hook.**

So closing the chat editor doesn't dispose the `IChatSession`, which means `_releaseSessionSubscription` is never called, which means the connection-layer refcount never reaches zero, which means we keep receiving full `ISessionState` delta envelopes (turns, tool calls, queued messages, etc.) for that session for the rest of the window's life.

## Scope

This is **not** a "subscribe to all running sessions on startup" problem — the session list view is fed by `listSessions()` summaries plus connection-level `SessionAdded` / `SessionRemoved` notifications, and never opens per-session subscriptions on its own. Only the chat content path (and now the running-session config picker provider) takes per-session subscriptions.

The cost grows linearly with the number of sessions the user has clicked into during a window's lifetime.

## Expected behavior

Drop the subscription when the chat editor closes, **but** keep it alive while the session is still running so background updates (tool-complete, turn-complete, status changes, auto-rename, etc.) continue to flow for sessions the user might come back to.

Concretely:

- **Chat editor closes** → if the session is in a terminal state, release immediately. Otherwise, arm a one-shot listener that releases on the next terminal-state transition.
- **Session reaches terminal state** → if the chat editor is already closed, release.

## Open question

What counts as "terminal"? `Done` / archived seems right. `Idle` is too aggressive — sessions sit `Idle` between turns and the user might be coming back to send another message in 30 seconds.

## Notes

- The connection-layer refcounting in `AgentSubscriptionManager` already supports this cleanly — this is purely a question of when the chat handler releases its ref.
- The `ChatSessionsService._sessions` cache lifecycle is a separate (related) question: today an `IChatSession` is created on first open and never invalidated when the editor closes. The fix here may need to either hook into editor-close at the `ChatSessionsService` level, or leave the `IChatSession` cached but have the handler scope the *subscription* lifetime more tightly than the `IChatSession` itself.

(Written by Copilot)
